market_physics · econophysics feature library · documentation

Market Physics

A Python library of physics-inspired quantitative features for financial time series. Each of its 137 indicators borrows a measurement concept from a branch of physics — mechanics, gravitation, electromagnetism, thermodynamics, information theory, chaos, fractal geometry, random matrix theory — and applies it as a descriptive estimator over OHLCV market data.

15 families 137 indicators all fully documented python 3.12+ pandas · numpy
Scope

Every output is a descriptive feature, not a trading signal. The library is not a buy/sell generator, not a strategy framework, and not a backtester. Physics names are analogies for the estimators — the library makes no claim that markets obey physical law, only that physical measurement concepts produce useful descriptive coordinates for market state.

Why econophysics features

Standard technical features describe price with price's own vocabulary: averages of it, ranges of it, ratios of it. Physics offers an alternative: a century of instruments built to measure any dynamical system — how disordered it is (entropy), how much energy a motion carries (mechanics), how strongly components couple (networks), whether fluctuations are self-similar (fractals), how far from equilibrium the system sits (thermodynamics). Applied to a return series, these instruments produce features that are often weakly correlated with classical technical indicators, which is precisely what makes them useful as inputs to research pipelines: they measure different axes of market state.

Each family chapter below opens with the physics — the original phenomenon, the governing relations, and how the estimator transplants them to market data — then documents every indicator: signature, physical picture, parameters, return contract, a runnable example, and the indicator computed on a shared reference dataset.

One contract, every indicator

Indicators take a pandas OHLCV dataframe (columns open, high, low, close, volume — most functions need only a subset) plus parameters, and return an IndicatorResult:

from market_physics.dynamics import market_velocity

result = market_velocity(df)
result.name        # "market_velocity"
result.values      # pd.Series aligned to df.index, NaN during warm-up
result.metadata    # parameters used + "formula" + "interpretation"
fieldtypewhat it holds
namestrIndicator identifier
valuespd.SeriesComputed series, index-aligned to the input; NaN during the warm-up window
metadatadict[str, Any]Exact parameters used, plus a formula string and a plain-language interpretation

One exception — gravity_levels returns a plain DataFrame of detected levels (one row per level) rather than a time series; see its entry.

Conventions & errors

  • Warm-up — a rolling indicator with window w is NaN until enough observations exist; output stays aligned to the input index.
  • Returns — functions with a return_method parameter accept "pct" (percentage change, default) or "log" (log returns).
  • Windows are bars — every window, lookback, and period is measured in bars/candles of whatever timeframe you feed in, never clock time.
  • Validation — missing columns raise MissingColumnError; too few rows raise InsufficientDataError (both subclass MarketPhysicsError from market_physics.exceptions); out-of-range parameters raise ValueError.
  • Histogram bins — distribution-based indicators accept bins as "fd" (Freedman–Diaconis, default), "sqrt", "sturges", or an integer ≥ 2.

The reference dataset

Every chart in this documentation shows the indicator computed on the same synthetic OHLCV series: 1200 bars through four engineered regimes — a low-volatility uptrend (bars 0–400), sideways chop (400–700), a high-volatility crash with a volume surge (700–850, shaded red), and a decaying-volatility recovery (850–1200). The networks family runs on a companion 8-asset returns panel driven by the same market factor, with cross-asset correlation engineered per regime: 0.30 in the uptrend, 0.25 in the chop, 0.80 in the crash, 0.45 in the recovery — so the many-body indicators face a textbook correlation spike. Synthetic data is used deliberately: because the regimes are known by construction, you can verify that each indicator responds the way its physics says it should.

dynamics

Newtonian kinematics — velocity, force, work · 10 indicators

Classical mechanics begins with a single mapping: a body has a position x(t), and everything else — velocity, acceleration, momentum, force — is derived from how that position changes. The dynamics family makes the same move for markets: price is position. Once x(t) = P(t), the whole kinematic chain follows by differentiation:

v(t) = dx/dt  →  return of the close price
a(t) = dv/dt  →  change in the return
p(t) = m·v(t)  →  volume-weighted return (volume plays mass)
F(t) = m·a(t)  →  volume-weighted change in return

The interesting physics is in the role of mass. In mechanics, mass is the body's resistance to acceleration; here volume is the mass of a move. A 2% move on heavy volume carries more momentum — in the literal p = mv sense — than the same move on thin volume, and by Newton's second law it takes a proportionally larger force to reverse it. This is why the family distinguishes velocity (how fast price moves) from momentum and force (how much conviction is behind the motion).

Beyond the kinematic chain, the family borrows from oscillator theory and energetics. A damped oscillator loses amplitude at a rate set by its damping ratio; a trend that keeps shrinking in amplitude behaves the same way. Friction is motion lost to resistance; inertia is the tendency of a trend to persist; work (W = F·d) is what volume expends pushing price over a distance; and potential energy accumulates when price is displaced from its equilibrium — a compressed spring waiting to release.

When to reach for this family: trend diagnosis. It answers, in a common mechanical vocabulary, how fast the market is moving, whether the motion is speeding up or decaying, and how much mass is behind it.

damping_ratioimpulse_energymarket_accelerationmarket_forcemarket_frictionmarket_momentummarket_potential_energymarket_velocitytrend_inertiawork_done_by_volume

damping_ratio

from market_physics.dynamics import damping_ratio

damping_ratio(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 100,
    *,
    short_window: int = 20,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
) -> IndicatorResult

Damping Ratio borrows the language of damped oscillators, where damping describes how quickly oscillation amplitude decays relative to a reference. Mapping price to position, it compares the short-horizon amplitude of returns against the long-horizon amplitude:

* short_amp -> rolling standard deviation of returns over short_window, * long_amp -> rolling standard deviation of returns over window, * ratio -> short_amp / long_amp.

Values below 1 describe short-term amplitude that is small relative to the longer horizon (recent oscillation is damped); values above 1 describe short-term amplitude that exceeds the longer horizon (recent oscillation is amplified). This is a descriptive amplitude ratio only — it is not a trading signal and carries no directional recommendation.

core quantity std(returns, short_window) / std(returns, window)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Only the close column is required.
windowint100Long-horizon rolling window for the reference amplitude (>= 2, and strictly greater than short_window).
short_window kw-onlyint20Short-horizon rolling window for the recent amplitude (>= 2 and less than window).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the result is undefined (e.g. zero long-horizon amplitude).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window or short_window is less than 2, short_window is not less than window, or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf the close column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.dynamics import damping_ratio

result = damping_ratio(df)
result.name                    # "damping_ratio"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "std(returns, short_window) / std(returns, window)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.88357 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 100 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Descriptive amplitude ratio: values below 1 describe short-term amplitude that is small relative to the longer horizon (recent oscillation damped) and values above 1 describe short-term amplitude that exceeds it (recent oscillation amplified). Descriptive only — not a signal.

impulse_energy

from market_physics.dynamics import impulse_energy

impulse_energy(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
    scale: float = 1.0,
) -> IndicatorResult

Impulse Energy borrows the mechanics idea of an impulse — a large force delivered over a short interval that produces a sudden change in motion. Mapping price to position, it measures how sharp a move is relative to its own recent volatility and scales that shock by the participating mass:

* shock -> abs(returns) / rolling_std(returns) (move size in units of its own recent volatility), * mass -> normalised volume (current volume relative to its rolling mean), * impulse -> shock * mass * scale.

Impulse Energy is **unsigned**: a sharp up-move and an equally sharp down-move of the same standardised magnitude and volume produce the same impulse. Higher values describe sudden, volume-backed shocks; values near zero describe calm, low-participation movement. This is a descriptive feature only — it is not a trading signal and carries no directional recommendation.

core quantity (abs(returns) / rolling_std(returns, window)) * normalized_volume * scale

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires the close and volume columns.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling volatility and rolling volume mean (>= 2).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
scale kw-onlyfloat1.0Positive multiplier applied to the final series for easier visualisation. Does not alter the relative shape of the values.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the result is undefined (e.g. zero rolling volatility or zero rolling volume mean).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2, scale is not greater than 0, or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close or volume is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.dynamics import impulse_energy

result = impulse_energy(df)
result.name                    # "impulse_energy"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "(abs(returns) / rolling_std(returns, window)) * normalized_volume * scale"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 1.111658 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 50 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Unsigned descriptive impulse: higher values describe sudden, volume-backed shocks (a move that is large relative to its own recent volatility, backed by above-average participation) and values near zero describe calm, low-participation movement. Descriptive only — not a signal.

market_acceleration

from market_physics.dynamics import market_acceleration

market_acceleration(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 1,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
    smooth: bool = False,
) -> IndicatorResult

Market Acceleration is the classical-mechanics analogue of acceleration — the rate of change of velocity. Mapping price to position, velocity is the close-price return and acceleration is the change in that return from one bar to the next.

Positive acceleration describes velocity increasing (price motion speeding up in the positive direction or decelerating downward); negative describes the opposite. This is a descriptive second-order rate of change only — it is not a trading signal and carries no directional recommendation.

core quantity diff(returns)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Only the close column is required.
windowint1Rolling window for smoothing (>= 1); ignored unless smooth is True.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
smooth kw-onlyboolFalseIf True, average the acceleration over window observations.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 1 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf the close column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined output (3 when not smoothing, window + 2 when smoothing).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.dynamics import market_acceleration

result = market_acceleration(df)
result.name                    # "market_acceleration"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "diff(returns)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.000495 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 2 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Positive acceleration describes velocity increasing and negative describes velocity decreasing; the magnitude describes how quickly velocity is changing. Descriptive only — not a signal.

market_force

from market_physics.dynamics import market_force

market_force(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 20,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
    scale: float = 1.0,
) -> IndicatorResult

Market Force is the classical-mechanics analogue of Newton's second law F = m * a — mass times acceleration. Market mapping:

* a (acceleration) -> change in close-price return (the first difference of velocity), * m (mass) -> normalised volume (current volume relative to its rolling mean), * F (force) -> normalized_volume * acceleration * scale.

Force is **signed**: positive values describe volume-backed acceleration in the positive direction and negative values describe the opposite. It is a descriptive feature only — not a trading signal and not a directional recommendation.

core quantity normalized_volume * diff(returns) * scale

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires the close and volume columns.
windowint20Lookback window for the rolling volume mean (>= 2).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
scale kw-onlyfloat1.0Positive multiplier applied to the final series for easier visualisation. Does not alter the relative shape of the values.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the result is undefined (e.g. zero rolling volume mean).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2, scale is not greater than 0, or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close or volume is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than max(window, 3) rows (enough for both the acceleration and the rolling mass).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.dynamics import market_force

result = market_force(df)
result.name                    # "market_force"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "normalized_volume * diff(returns) * scale"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.006968 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 19 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Signed descriptive force: positive values describe volume-backed acceleration in the positive direction and negative values describe the opposite. Descriptive only — not a signal.

market_friction

from market_physics.dynamics import market_friction

market_friction(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
) -> IndicatorResult

Market Friction borrows the idea of a path that dissipates energy through resistance. Mapping price to position, it compares the total distance travelled by the return path against the net displacement over the same window:

* path_length -> sum of absolute returns (total ground covered), * net_displacement -> absolute sum of returns (straight-line progress), * friction -> 1 - net_displacement / path_length.

The value lives in [0, 1]. Near 0 describes efficient, directional movement (the path is nearly a straight line and almost all motion translates into net displacement). Near 1 describes noisy movement with little net displacement (the path wanders and cancels out, like motion lost to friction).

This is a descriptive feature only — it is not a trading signal and carries no directional recommendation.

core quantity 1 - abs(sum(returns, window)) / sum(abs(returns), window)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Only the close column is required.
windowint50Rolling window over which the path is measured (>= 2).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the result is undefined (e.g. zero path length over a flat window).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf the close column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.dynamics import market_friction

result = market_friction(df)
result.name                    # "market_friction"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "1 - abs(sum(returns, window)) / sum(abs(returns), window)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.926859 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 50 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Descriptive path-efficiency measure in [0, 1]: near 0 describes efficient, directional movement (net displacement close to the path length) and near 1 describes noisy movement with little net displacement. Descriptive only — not a signal.

market_momentum

from market_physics.dynamics import market_momentum

market_momentum(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 20,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
    scale: float = 1.0,
) -> IndicatorResult

Market Momentum is the classical-mechanics analogue of momentum p = m * v — mass times velocity. Market mapping:

* v (velocity) -> close-price return, * m (mass) -> normalised volume (current volume relative to its rolling mean), * p (momentum) -> normalized_volume * return * scale.

Unlike :func:`market_physics.energy.momentum_energy` (which squares the return and is therefore unsigned), this momentum is **signed**: positive values describe volume-backed upward motion and negative values describe volume-backed downward motion. It is a descriptive feature only — not the same quantity as momentum energy, and not a trading signal.

core quantity normalized_volume * returns * scale

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires the close and volume columns.
windowint20Lookback window for the rolling volume mean (>= 2).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute returns ("velocity"). "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
scale kw-onlyfloat1.0Positive multiplier applied to the final series for easier visualisation. Does not alter the relative shape of the values.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the result is undefined (e.g. zero rolling volume mean).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2, scale is not greater than 0, or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close or volume is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.dynamics import market_momentum

result = market_momentum(df)
result.name                    # "market_momentum"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "normalized_volume * returns * scale"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.001937 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 19 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Signed descriptive momentum: positive values describe volume-backed upward motion and negative values describe volume-backed downward motion. Distinct from momentum_energy (which is unsigned). Descriptive only — not a signal.

market_potential_energy

from market_physics.dynamics import market_potential_energy

market_potential_energy(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 100,
    *,
    equilibrium: Literal['vwap', 'sma'] = 'vwap',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Market Potential Energy borrows the idea of the potential energy stored in a displacement from equilibrium (as in a spring, U = 0.5 * k * x^2). Mapping price to position, the equilibrium is a rolling centre of price and the displacement is how far the current close sits from that centre:

* center -> a rolling equilibrium price ("sma" rolling mean or "vwap" volume-weighted average price), * displacement -> close - center, * potential -> displacement ** 2 (optionally divided by center ** 2 when normalize).

Market Potential Energy is **unsigned**: it grows with the squared distance from equilibrium regardless of which side of the centre price sits on. It describes how stretched price is relative to its rolling centre; it is explicitly **not** a mean-reversion signal and implies no expectation that price will return to the centre. This is a descriptive feature only — it is not a trading signal and carries no directional recommendation.

core quantity (close - center) ** 2 / center ** 2

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires close; also requires volume when equilibrium is "vwap".
windowint100Lookback window for the rolling equilibrium (>= 2).
equilibrium kw-onlyLiteral['vwap', 'sma']'vwap'Which rolling centre to use. "sma" uses the rolling mean of close; "vwap" uses the rolling volume-weighted average price.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the squared displacement by center ** 2 to produce a scale-free measure.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the result is undefined (e.g. zero rolling volume for "vwap" or a zero centre when normalising).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or equilibrium is not one of "vwap" or "sma".
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing (close, plus volume when equilibrium is "vwap").
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.dynamics import market_potential_energy

result = market_potential_energy(df)
result.name                    # "market_potential_energy"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "(close - center) ** 2 / center ** 2"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.001258 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 99 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Unsigned descriptive measure of how far price is displaced from its rolling equilibrium centre: larger values describe price that is more stretched from the centre, regardless of side. Not a mean-reversion signal — it implies no expectation that price will return to the centre. Descriptive only — not a signal.

market_velocity

from market_physics.dynamics import market_velocity

market_velocity(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 1,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
    smooth: bool = False,
) -> IndicatorResult

Market Velocity is the classical-mechanics analogue of velocity — the rate of change of position. Mapping price to position, velocity is simply the return of the close price over the period.

Positive velocity describes upward price velocity over the period and negative velocity describes downward; the magnitude describes how fast price is moving. This is a descriptive rate of change only — it is not a trading signal and carries no directional recommendation.

core quantity returns

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Only the close column is required.
windowint1Rolling window for smoothing (>= 1); ignored unless smooth is True.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
smooth kw-onlyboolFalseIf True, average the velocity over window observations.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 1 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf the close column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined output (2 when not smoothing, window + 1 when smoothing).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.dynamics import market_velocity

result = market_velocity(df)
result.name                    # "market_velocity"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "returns"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # -0.000137 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 1 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Positive velocity describes upward price velocity over the period and negative velocity describes downward; the magnitude describes how fast price is moving. Descriptive only — not a signal.

trend_inertia

from market_physics.dynamics import trend_inertia

trend_inertia(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
    volume_weighted: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Trend Inertia borrows the classical-mechanics idea of inertia — a body's tendency to keep moving in the same direction. Mapping price to position, the return direction plays the role of the direction of motion; inertia measures how consistently that direction persists over the window.

When volume_weighted is False the value lives roughly in [0, 1]: high inertia describes a persistent directional regime (the return direction rarely flips), low inertia describes choppy, unstable direction (the sign flips often and cancels out). When volume_weighted is True, each direction is scaled by normalised volume so that high-participation bars contribute more to the persistence measure. Normalised volume acts as a mass/amplifier, so weighted values may exceed 1.0 when persistent direction coincides with above-average volume; weighted values are intentionally left unclipped so the amplification is preserved.

This is a descriptive feature only — it is not a trading signal and carries no directional recommendation.

core quantity abs(rolling_mean(sign(returns) * normalized_volume, window))

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires close; also requires volume when volume_weighted is True.
windowint50Rolling window for the direction persistence measure (>= 2).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
volume_weighted kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, weight each direction by normalised volume.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period. Unweighted values are roughly bounded in [0, 1]; volume-weighted values may exceed 1.0 because normalised volume amplifies persistent direction, and are intentionally not clipped.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing (close, plus volume when volume_weighted is True).
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined output (window + 1 when unweighted, 2 * window - 1 when volume weighted, to account for the nested rolling volume mean and rolling direction mean).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.dynamics import trend_inertia

result = trend_inertia(df)
result.name                    # "trend_inertia"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "abs(rolling_mean(sign(returns) * normalized_volume, window))"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.024825 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Descriptive measure of volume-amplified directional persistence: higher magnitudes describe a persistent directional regime backed by above-average volume and near-zero values describe choppy, unstable direction. Normalized volume acts as a mass/amplifier, so values may exceed 1.0 and are not clipped. Descriptive only — not a signal.

work_done_by_volume

from market_physics.dynamics import work_done_by_volume

work_done_by_volume(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
    cumulative: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Work Done by Volume borrows the mechanics definition of work — force applied over a displacement (W = F * d). Mapping price to position, the displacement is the return and the force is proxied by the participating mass (normalised volume):

* mass -> normalised volume (current volume relative to its rolling mean), * work_step -> mass * returns (signed per-bar work), * work -> the per-bar work, or its rolling sum when cumulative.

Work is **signed**: positive values describe volume-backed work in the positive direction and negative values describe volume-backed work in the negative direction; the rolling sum accumulates and can partially cancel. This is a descriptive feature only — it is not a trading signal and carries no directional recommendation.

core quantity rolling_sum(normalized_volume * returns, window)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires the close and volume columns.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling volume mean and, when cumulative, the rolling sum (>= 2).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
cumulative kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, accumulate the per-bar work over window via a rolling sum; if False, return the signed per-bar work.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the result is undefined (e.g. zero rolling volume mean).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close or volume is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined output (window when not cumulative, 2 * window - 1 when cumulative, to account for the nested rolling volume mean and rolling sum).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.dynamics import work_done_by_volume

result = work_done_by_volume(df)
result.name                    # "work_done_by_volume"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "rolling_sum(normalized_volume * returns, window)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.032724 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Signed descriptive work: positive values describe volume-backed work in the positive direction and negative values describe volume-backed work in the negative direction; the rolling sum accumulates and can partially cancel. Descriptive only — not a signal.

gravity

Gravitation — mass, potential wells, orbits · 7 indicators

Newtonian gravity says mass curves the space around it: a body of mass M creates a potential U(r) = −GM/r, and objects roll downhill toward it. The gravity family transplants this to the price axis. Traded volume at a price level is mass: a level where a large amount of volume changed hands is a massive body sitting on the price line, and it attracts price the way a planet attracts a satellite.

potential at price P  =  Σi mi / (|P − Li| + ε)    — mi volume mass at level Li

The physical intuition is behavioural: at a heavily-traded level, many positions were opened. Those positions are memory — resting orders, break-even stops, anchored expectations — and they pull price back when it wanders. The family builds this in two steps. First, level detection: histogram the traded volume across price bins over a lookback, and pick out the local maxima of the mass distribution (gravity_levels, gravity_wells — the wells being the potential minima price tends to settle into). Second, field measurement: given the mass distribution, compute the potential at the current price, its gradient (the net pull, signed toward the dominant mass), the balance between masses above and below, and how far price has strayed from the nearest well — its orbital deviation.

Readers who know market-profile analysis will recognise high-volume nodes; the gravity framing adds the field formalism — a scalar potential, a gradient, an equilibrium — so the distance to a level and the strength of its pull combine into one number.

When to reach for this family: support/resistance in continuous form. Instead of binary "level broken / level held", you get a smooth potential landscape and price's position within it.

field_gradientfield_potentialgravitational_balancegravity_levelsgravity_wellsmass_concentration_scoreorbital_deviation

field_gradient

from market_physics.gravity import field_gradient

field_gradient(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    lookback: int = 200,
    *,
    price_bins: int = 50,
    gravity_constant: float = 1.0,
    epsilon: float = 1e-09,
    normalize: bool = True,
    price_source: Literal['close', 'range'] = 'close',
) -> IndicatorResult

Physical analogy ———————— The Field Gradient is the bar-to-bar change in the net gravitational field that :func:`market_physics.gravity.gravity_wells.gravity_wells` computes. Where that field describes the current net pull on price, its gradient describes how that pull is *changing*:

field = net signed inverse-square field of the rolling volume profile gradient = field.diff()

The net field is produced by the shared :func:`market_physics.gravity._field` helper — the same calculation gravity_wells uses — so the two stay consistent. Because the gradient is a first difference, its first defined value requires two consecutive field values; the warm-up therefore extends one bar beyond the field's own warm-up.

This indicator is **descriptive only**: it expresses the rate of change of a field value, not a buy/sell signal.

Interpretation:

* positive gradient -> the field is becoming more positive (upward-pull- dominant relative to the previous bar), * negative gradient -> the field is becoming more negative (downward-pull- dominant relative to the previous bar), * near zero -> the field is relatively stable.

core quantity diff(net_gravitational_field)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires close/volume (price_source "close") or high/low/close/volume (price_source "range").
lookbackint200Number of observations in each rolling window (>= 10).
price_bins kw-onlyint50Number of price levels in the volume profile (>= 2).
gravity_constant kw-onlyfloat1.0Gravitational constant G scaling every force.
epsilon kw-onlyfloat1e-09Lower bound for the softening term added to distance^2.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, normalise the underlying field before differencing.
price_source kw-onlyLiteral['close', 'range']'close'"close" assigns each candle's volume to its close bin; "range" distributes it across the bins overlapping the candle's low-high range.
Returns

An IndicatorResult whose values series is aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period. The first defined value appears one bar after the field's own warm-up (the difference needs two consecutive field values).

Raises
ValueErrorIf lookback, price_bins, gravity_constant, epsilon or price_source is out of range.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than lookback + 1 rows (two field values are needed to form one gradient).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.gravity import field_gradient

result = field_gradient(df)
result.name                    # "field_gradient"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "diff(net_gravitational_field)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.01143 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 200 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — positive means the field is becoming more positive (upward-pull-dominant vs the previous bar), negative means more negative (downward-pull-dominant), near zero is stable

field_potential

from market_physics.gravity import field_potential

field_potential(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    lookback: int = 200,
    *,
    price_bins: int = 50,
    price_source: Literal['close', 'range'] = 'range',
    normalize: bool = True,
    center: Literal['center_of_mass', 'dominant'] = 'center_of_mass',
) -> IndicatorResult

Physical analogy ———————— In a gravitational (or spring) field, the potential energy of a body grows with the square of its displacement from equilibrium. Mapping that onto markets, the equilibrium is the volume-mass center of a rolling window and the displacement is how far the current price sits from it:

displacement = current_close - center_price potential = displacement ** 2

optionally divided by current_close ** 2 to give a simple, scale-free relative measure. The mass center is either the mass-weighted average of bin centers (center="center_of_mass") or the center of the highest-mass bin (center="dominant").

For each rolling lookback window a volume profile is built over price_bins price levels (see price_source).

This indicator is **descriptive only**. It measures how far price sits from a recent volume-mass equilibrium; it makes **no mean-reversion claim** and implies no expectation that price will move back toward the center.

Interpretation:

* higher -> price is farther from the recent volume-mass equilibrium, * lower -> price is closer to the recent volume-mass equilibrium.

core quantity (current_close - center_price) ** 2 / current_close ** 2

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires close/volume (price_source "close") or high/low/close/volume (price_source "range").
lookbackint200Number of observations in each rolling window (>= 10).
price_bins kw-onlyint50Number of price levels in the volume profile (>= 2).
price_source kw-onlyLiteral['close', 'range']'range'"close" assigns each candle's volume to its close bin; "range" distributes it across the bins overlapping the candle's low-high range.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the squared displacement by current_close ** 2.
center kw-onlyLiteral['center_of_mass', 'dominant']'center_of_mass'"center_of_mass" uses the mass-weighted average of bin centers; "dominant" uses the center of the highest-mass bin.
Returns

An IndicatorResult whose values series is aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and for windows with no usable mass.

Raises
ValueErrorIf lookback, price_bins, price_source or center is out of range.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than lookback rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.gravity import field_potential

result = field_potential(df)
result.name                    # "field_potential"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "(current_close - center_price) ** 2 / current_close ** 2"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.013196 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 199 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — higher means price is farther from the recent volume-mass equilibrium, lower means closer. Not a mean-reversion claim

gravitational_balance

from market_physics.gravity import gravitational_balance

gravitational_balance(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    lookback: int = 200,
    *,
    price_bins: int = 50,
    price_source: Literal['close', 'range'] = 'range',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Physical analogy ———————— If high-volume price zones act like masses, then the current price sits inside a field whose mass is distributed above and below it. Gravitational Balance measures how that mass is split relative to the current price:

balance = mass_above - mass_below

optionally normalised by the total profile mass so the value lands roughly in [-1, 1].

For each rolling lookback window a volume profile is built over price_bins price levels (see price_source). Each bin is classified as *above* or *below* the current (latest close) price by comparing the bin's center to the current price; a bin whose center coincides exactly with the current price (a measure-zero, rare case) is treated neutrally and excluded from both sides. Because |mass_above - mass_below| never exceeds the total mass, the normalised value stays within [-1, 1].

This indicator is **descriptive only**: it summarises where volume mass sits relative to price, not a buy/sell signal.

Interpretation:

* positive value -> more volume mass above the current price, * negative value -> more volume mass below the current price, * near zero -> upper and lower mass are balanced.

core quantity (mass_above - mass_below) / total_mass

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires close/volume (price_source "close") or high/low/close/volume (price_source "range").
lookbackint200Number of observations in each rolling window (>= 10).
price_bins kw-onlyint50Number of price levels in the volume profile (>= 2).
price_source kw-onlyLiteral['close', 'range']'range'"close" assigns each candle's volume to its close bin; "range" distributes it across the bins overlapping the candle's low-high range.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the balance by the total profile mass so values land roughly in [-1, 1].
Returns

An IndicatorResult whose values series is aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and for windows with no usable mass.

Raises
ValueErrorIf lookback, price_bins or price_source is out of range.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than lookback rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.gravity import gravitational_balance

result = gravitational_balance(df)
result.name                    # "gravitational_balance"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "(mass_above - mass_below) / total_mass"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # -0.916884 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 199 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — positive means more mass above price, negative means more mass below price, near zero is balanced

gravity_levels

from market_physics.gravity import gravity_levels

gravity_levels(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    lookback: int = 500,
    *,
    price_bins: int = 80,
    top_n: int = 8,
    merge_threshold: float = 0.005,
    current_threshold: float = 0.002,
    candidate_multiplier: int = 3,
    price_source: Literal['close', 'range'] = 'range',
) -> pd.DataFrame

Gravity Levels utility.

Gravity Levels detects high-volume price zones that behave like gravitational mass concentrations. It builds a volume profile over a rolling lookback window — inspired by the idea that price levels where significant volume has traded act as "mass" — and surfaces the strongest discrete zones.

Unlike the time-series indicators in this package, gravity_levels returns a :class:`pandas.DataFrame`: levels are discrete price zones, not a value per timestamp.

This is a **descriptive** level-detection utility, not a buy/sell signal, strategy, or backtest.

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires close/volume (price_source "close") or high/low/close/volume (price_source "range").
lookbackint500Number of most-recent rows used to build the profile.
price_bins kw-onlyint80Number of price levels in the volume profile.
top_n kw-onlyint8Maximum number of levels to return.
merge_threshold kw-onlyfloat0.005Relative distance below which adjacent levels merge.
current_threshold kw-onlyfloat0.002Relative distance to the latest close within which a level is labelled "current" rather than support/resistance.
candidate_multiplier kw-onlyint3Multiplier on top_n controlling how many of the strongest bins are considered before merging. Larger values let more nearby medium-strength bins merge into stronger zones.
price_source kw-onlyLiteral['close', 'range']'range'"close" assigns each candle's volume to its close bin; "range" distributes it across the bins overlapping the candle's low-high range.
Returns

A DataFrame with columns rank, price, mass, strength, mass_share, side, distance_pct and zone_width_pct (one row per level, strongest first). Empty (with those columns) if no valid levels are found.

Raises
ValueErrorIf lookback, price_bins, top_n, merge_threshold, current_threshold, candidate_multiplier or price_source are out of range.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than lookback rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.gravity import gravity_levels

levels = gravity_levels(df)
levels.columns.tolist()
# ['rank', 'price', 'mass', 'strength', 'mass_share', 'side', 'distance_pct', 'zone_width_pct']
len(levels)  # 8 levels found on the synthetic dataset

Reading it — Each amber line is a detected level; opacity encodes gravitational strength. Columns: rank, price, mass, strength, mass_share, side, distance_pct, zone_width_pct.

gravity_wells

from market_physics.gravity import gravity_wells

gravity_wells(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    lookback: int = 200,
    *,
    price_bins: int = 50,
    gravity_constant: float = 1.0,
    epsilon: float = 1e-09,
    normalize: bool = True,
    price_source: Literal['close', 'range'] = 'close',
) -> IndicatorResult

Physical analogy ———————— A gravity well is a region of space where mass concentrates and pulls nearby bodies toward it. Mapping that onto markets:

* Price levels where significant volume has traded act as "masses". * The current price is the "body" experiencing attraction toward those high-mass price levels (think volume-profile points of control). * Closer high-volume zones exert a stronger pull, following an inverse-square style relationship.

For each rolling lookback window a volume profile is built over price_bins price levels. Each bin contributes a signed force toward the current (latest close) price:

signed_force = sign(distance) * G * mass / (distance^2 + softening)

where distance = bin_center - current_price and softening is max(epsilon, bin_width^2). The net field is the sum of those signed forces, optionally normalised by the total absolute force.

Volume profile sources (price_source):

* "close" - each candle's volume is assigned to the bin its close falls in (point-of-trade approximation). * "range" - each candle's volume is distributed equally across every bin overlapping its low-high range (range-of-trade approximation).

This indicator is **descriptive only**: it expresses a gravitational field value, not a buy/sell signal.

Interpretation:

* positive value -> stronger pull toward higher price zones, * negative value -> stronger pull toward lower price zones, * near zero -> balanced gravitational field.

core quantity signed_force = sign(distance) * G * mass / (distance^2 + softening)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires close/volume (price_source "close") or high/low/close/volume (price_source "range").
lookbackint200Number of observations in each rolling window.
price_bins kw-onlyint50Number of price levels in the volume profile.
gravity_constant kw-onlyfloat1.0Gravitational constant G scaling every force.
epsilon kw-onlyfloat1e-09Lower bound for the softening term added to distance^2.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the net field by the total absolute force so values land roughly in [-1, 1].
price_source kw-onlyLiteral['close', 'range']'close'"close" assigns each candle's volume to its close bin; "range" distributes it across the bins overlapping the candle's low-high range.
Returns

An IndicatorResult whose values series is aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period.

Raises
ValueErrorIf lookback, price_bins, gravity_constant, epsilon or price_source are out of range.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than lookback rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.gravity import gravity_wells

result = gravity_wells(df)
result.name                    # "gravity_wells"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "signed_force = sign(distance) * G * mass / (distance^2 + softening)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # -0.574084 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 199 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — positive pulls upward, negative pulls downward, near zero is balanced

mass_concentration_score

from market_physics.gravity import mass_concentration_score

mass_concentration_score(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    lookback: int = 200,
    *,
    price_bins: int = 50,
    price_source: Literal['close', 'range'] = 'range',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Physical analogy ———————— Volume mass can be tightly clustered in a few price zones or spread thinly across many. Mass Concentration Score summarises that dispersion with a Herfindahl-Hirschman-style concentration measure on the volume profile:

hhi = sum(p_i ** 2)

where p_i is the share of total mass in bin i. When normalised, the score is rescaled so that a perfectly uniform profile maps to 0 and a single-bin profile maps to 1:

normalized = (hhi - 1/k) / (1 - 1/k)

with k = price_bins (the total number of bins). Total bins are used rather than occupied bins so the denominator is stable across rolling windows. Since the minimum achievable hhi over k bins is 1/k (uniform mass), the normalised score stays within [0, 1].

This indicator is **descriptive only**: it summarises how concentrated volume mass is, not a buy/sell signal.

Interpretation:

* higher -> mass is concentrated in fewer price zones, * lower -> mass is spread across many price zones.

core quantity (sum(p_i ** 2) - 1/k) / (1 - 1/k)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires close/volume (price_source "close") or high/low/close/volume (price_source "range").
lookbackint200Number of observations in each rolling window (>= 10).
price_bins kw-onlyint50Number of price levels in the volume profile (>= 2).
price_source kw-onlyLiteral['close', 'range']'range'"close" assigns each candle's volume to its close bin; "range" distributes it across the bins overlapping the candle's low-high range.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, rescale the index so a uniform profile maps to 0 and a single-bin profile maps to 1.
Returns

An IndicatorResult whose values series is aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and for windows with no usable mass.

Raises
ValueErrorIf lookback, price_bins or price_source is out of range.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than lookback rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.gravity import mass_concentration_score

result = mass_concentration_score(df)
result.name                    # "mass_concentration_score"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "(sum(p_i ** 2) - 1/k) / (1 - 1/k)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.009336 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 199 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — higher means mass concentrated in fewer price zones, lower means mass spread across price zones

orbital_deviation

from market_physics.gravity import orbital_deviation

orbital_deviation(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    lookback: int = 200,
    *,
    price_bins: int = 50,
    price_source: Literal['close', 'range'] = 'range',
    center: Literal['dominant', 'center_of_mass'] = 'dominant',
    normalize: bool = True,
    signed: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Physical analogy ———————— If a high-mass volume zone acts as a gravitational center, the current price can be thought of as a body displaced from that center's "orbit". Orbital Deviation measures that displacement:

deviation = current_close - center_price

where center_price is either the center of the highest-mass bin (center="dominant") or the mass-weighted average of all bin centers (center="center_of_mass"). When normalize is True the deviation is divided by the current close, giving a simple relative displacement. When signed is False the absolute deviation is used.

For each rolling lookback window a volume profile is built over price_bins price levels (see price_source).

This indicator is **descriptive only**: it summarises how far price sits from a mass center. It makes no mean-reversion claim and implies no expectation that price will move back toward the center.

Interpretation:

* signed positive -> price is above the mass center, * signed negative -> price is below the mass center, * magnitude -> distance from the dominant / center-of-mass zone.

core quantity (current_close - center_price) / current_close

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires close/volume (price_source "close") or high/low/close/volume (price_source "range").
lookbackint200Number of observations in each rolling window (>= 10).
price_bins kw-onlyint50Number of price levels in the volume profile (>= 2).
price_source kw-onlyLiteral['close', 'range']'range'"close" assigns each candle's volume to its close bin; "range" distributes it across the bins overlapping the candle's low-high range.
center kw-onlyLiteral['dominant', 'center_of_mass']'dominant'"dominant" uses the center of the highest-mass bin; "center_of_mass" uses the mass-weighted average of bin centers.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the deviation by the current close.
signed kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, keep the sign of the deviation; if False, use its absolute value.
Returns

An IndicatorResult whose values series is aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and for windows with no usable mass.

Raises
ValueErrorIf lookback, price_bins, price_source or center is out of range.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than lookback rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.gravity import orbital_deviation

result = orbital_deviation(df)
result.name                    # "orbital_deviation"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "(current_close - center_price) / current_close"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.026656 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 199 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — signed positive means price above the mass center, signed negative means below; magnitude is distance from the center. Not a mean-reversion claim

fields

Electromagnetism — charge, current, induction · 9 indicators

Electromagnetism is the physics of charge in motion. A static charge creates an electric field (Coulomb); moving charge is a current that creates a magnetic field (Ampère); and a changing magnetic flux induces a voltage that opposes the change that made it (Faraday–Lenz). The fields family maps order-flow imbalance onto this structure: signed buying/selling pressure is charge.

q(t)  =  sign of the move × volume   — market charge
I(t)  =  dq/dt   — market current: the flow of charge
u ∝ E² + B²   — field energy density → electromagnetic pressure

From that one identification the rest of the vocabulary follows. Charge density is imbalance concentrated per unit time — sustained one-sided pressure rather than a single burst. Polarity is the sign structure of recent charge: a market flipping between positive and negative charge is depolarised; one accumulating same-signed charge is strongly polarised. Current is charge in motion — directional volume flow — and like a physical current it does work and dissipates. Induction is the Faraday move: when the flow changes rapidly, the market induces a response that resists the change (liquidity stepping in against an aggressive burst). Electromagnetic pressure sums the squared field strengths into an energy density — how much stress the combined fields put on the current price.

Where the dynamics family asks how price moves, the fields family asks how the order flow behind it is charged — its sign, concentration, flow rate, and the stresses those create.

When to reach for this family: pressure and imbalance diagnosis — detecting sustained one-sided flow, polarity flips, and aggressive bursts that the market is likely to resist.

charge_densityelectric_field_strengthelectromagnetic_pressurefield_interaction_scoremagnetic_flowmarket_chargemarket_currentmarket_inductionpolarity_index

charge_density

from market_physics.fields import charge_density

charge_density(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    mode: Literal['absolute', 'signed'] = 'absolute',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

This indicator summarises how much signed charge accumulates locally per bar. The per-bar charge contribution is returns * normalized_volume. In "absolute" mode it aggregates the unsigned charge (how much charge is flowing regardless of direction); in "signed" mode it preserves polarity (net charge). Normalised aggregation uses a rolling mean; unnormalised uses a rolling sum.

Known relationship: in signed mode, normalize=False equals market_charge(normalize=False) at matched parameters, and normalize=True is the rolling-mean version of the same signed charge step. It is computed locally and does not import that indicator.

The indicator is a **descriptive proxy only**: it is an MVP local charge-density feature, not a physical charge density, and not where price will go next.

core quantity rolling_mean(abs(charge_step)); charge_step = returns * normalized_volume

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—Dataframe with close and volume columns.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling aggregation. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
mode kw-onlyLiteral['absolute', 'signed']'absolute'"absolute" for unsigned density; "signed" to preserve polarity.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, use a rolling mean; otherwise a rolling sum.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2, return_method is invalid, or mode is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than 2 * window - 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.fields import charge_density

result = charge_density(df)
result.name                    # "charge_density"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "rolling_mean(abs(charge_step)); charge_step = returns * normalized_volume"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.009626 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — In 'absolute' mode this is the unsigned local charge density (how much charge flows regardless of direction); in 'signed' mode it preserves polarity (net charge). Descriptive only.

electric_field_strength

from market_physics.fields import electric_field_strength

electric_field_strength(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the idea of a field intensity, this indicator measures the average signed charge contribution over a window relative to its own dispersion: a strong, consistent signed charge reads as a strong field, while noisy or balanced charge reads as a weak field. The signed charge contribution is returns * normalized_volume.

The indicator is a **descriptive proxy only**: it is an electric-field-*style* feature, not a physical electric field, and not where price will go next.

core quantity rolling_mean(returns * normalized_volume) / rolling_std(returns * normalized_volume)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—Dataframe with close and volume columns.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, standardise the raw field by its rolling dispersion.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than 2 * window - 1 rows (returns lose the first row and normalised volume needs a full window before the outer rolling statistics).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.fields import electric_field_strength

result = electric_field_strength(df)
result.name                    # "electric_field_strength"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "rolling_mean(returns * normalized_volume) / rolling_std(returns * normalized_volume)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.044814 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Positive values indicate a net positive (up-move) signed field; negative values a net negative (down-move) field; larger magnitude means a stronger relative signed field. Descriptive only.

electromagnetic_pressure

from market_physics.fields import electromagnetic_pressure

electromagnetic_pressure(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    min_valid_components: int = 2,
) -> IndicatorResult

This composite blends four bounded field-interaction magnitudes into a single pressure-like score: the absolute market charge (signed imbalance), the charge density (unsigned magnitude of signed volume participation), the magnetic flow (current variability), and the market induction. Each is mapped into [0, 1] and the score is the mean of the components that are defined.

Because the components are built from progressively deeper rolling constructions (charge, its differenced current, and their rolling statistics), they become valid at different times; a bar is scored only when at least min_valid_components of them are finite.

The indicator is a **descriptive composite proxy only**: it is not a classifier, not a trading signal, and not a claim about what comes next.

core quantity mean of abs(market_charge), bounded(rolling_mean(abs(charge_step))), bounded(magnetic_flow), and bounded(abs(market_induction)); bounded(x) = abs(x) / (1 + abs(x)); requires at least min_valid_components finite components

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—Dataframe with close and volume columns.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
min_valid_components kw-onlyint2Minimum number of finite components required to score a bar. Between 1 and 4.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in approximately [0, 1], NaN during the warm-up period and wherever fewer than min_valid_components components are defined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2, return_method is invalid, or min_valid_components is not between 1 and 4.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than the min_valid_components-th component needs.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.fields import electromagnetic_pressure

result = electromagnetic_pressure(df)
result.name                    # "electromagnetic_pressure"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "mean of abs(market_charge), bounded(rolling_mean(abs(charge_step))), bounded(magnetic_flow), and bounded(abs(market_induction)); bounded(x) = abs(x) / (1 + abs(x)); requires at least min_valid_components finite components"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.202303 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate a blend of stronger charge imbalance, charge density, current variability and induction magnitudes; lower values indicate a calmer field state. Descriptive proxy only.

field_interaction_score

from market_physics.fields import field_interaction_score

field_interaction_score(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

This indicator measures how consistently the bar-by-bar polarity aligns with volume participation over a window. When up/down moves on heavy volume pull in the same direction, the interaction is strong (near 1); when they cancel, it is weak (near 0). Polarity is sign(returns) (flat bars contribute 0) and volume participation is volume / rolling_mean(volume).

Known relationship: normalised mode equals the absolute value of the volume-weighted polarity_index at matched parameters; raw mode is a signed polarity-volume interaction closely related to the volume-weighted polarity_index. It is computed locally from polarity and volume-participation components and does not import that indicator.

The indicator is a **descriptive proxy only**: it is a field-interaction-*style* feature, not a physical electromagnetic field, and not where price will go next.

core quantity abs(rolling_sum(sign(returns) * volume_participation)) / rolling_sum(volume_participation)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—Dataframe with close and volume columns.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, return the bounded [0, 1] alignment score.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than 2 * window - 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.fields import field_interaction_score

result = field_interaction_score(df)
result.name                    # "field_interaction_score"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "abs(rolling_sum(sign(returns) * volume_participation)) / rolling_sum(volume_participation)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.024038 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate stronger persistent polarity-volume alignment; lower values indicate mixed or cancelling interaction. Descriptive only.

magnetic_flow

from market_physics.fields import magnetic_flow

magnetic_flow(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the idea that a changing current is associated with magnetic effects, this indicator measures the variability (circulation) of the signed charge current over a window: a current that swings around a lot reads as strong magnetic flow, while a steady current reads as weak. The charge and its current are built from internal charge components (current = normalized_charge.diff()).

The indicator is a **descriptive proxy only**: it is a magnetic-flow-*style* feature, not physical magnetism, and not where price will go next.

core quantity rolling_std(normalized_charge.diff()) / rolling_mean(abs(normalized_charge.diff()))

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—Dataframe with close and volume columns.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, express the current variability relative to the mean current magnitude.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than 3 * window - 1 rows (the charge, its differenced current, and the outer rolling std stack three window-length warm-ups minus overlaps).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.fields import magnetic_flow

result = magnetic_flow(df)
result.name                    # "magnetic_flow"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "rolling_std(normalized_charge.diff()) / rolling_mean(abs(normalized_charge.diff()))"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 1.332472 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 148 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate stronger variability/circulation in the signed charge current; lower values indicate a steadier current. Descriptive only.

market_charge

from market_physics.fields import market_charge

market_charge(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the idea of electric charge as a signed accumulation, this indicator accumulates volume-weighted signed returns over a window: up moves on heavy volume add positive charge, down moves on heavy volume add negative charge. When normalised, the net charge is expressed as a fraction of the total charge magnitude, giving a signed imbalance in [-1, 1].

Known relationship: when normalised at matched parameters this is equivalent to turbulence.market_flow(normalize=True), but here it is interpreted as a signed charge imbalance rather than a flow. It is computed locally and does not import that indicator.

The indicator is a **descriptive proxy only**: it is an electromagnetic charge-*style* feature, not physical charge, and not where price will go next.

core quantity rolling_sum(returns * normalized_volume) / rolling_sum(abs(returns * normalized_volume))

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—Dataframe with close and volume columns.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling sums. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, express the charge as a fraction of total charge magnitude (a signed imbalance in [-1, 1]).
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than 2 * window - 1 rows (returns lose the first row and normalised volume needs a full window before the outer rolling sum).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.fields import market_charge

result = market_charge(df)
result.name                    # "market_charge"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "rolling_sum(returns * normalized_volume) / rolling_sum(abs(returns * normalized_volume))"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.061099 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Positive values indicate a net positive (up-move) charge imbalance; negative values a net negative (down-move) imbalance; values near zero a balanced charge. Descriptive only.

market_current

from market_physics.fields import market_current

market_current(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    current_window: int = 1,
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the idea that electric current is the rate of change of charge, this indicator reports how fast the market charge is changing over current_window bars. When normalised, the change is standardised by the rolling volatility of the charge, so it reads like a z-scored charge-state change.

Known relationship: this is equal to turbulence.flow_velocity when both are normalized at matched parameters; it differs in unnormalized mode, where the charge remains normalized before differencing. Here it is interpreted as a charge-state current, computed locally and without importing that indicator.

The indicator is a **descriptive proxy only**: it is an electromagnetic current-*style* feature, not physical current, and not where price will go next.

core quantity market_charge.diff(current_window) / rolling_std(market_charge)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—Dataframe with close and volume columns.
windowint50Lookback window for the charge and its rolling std. At least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
current_window kw-onlyint1Number of bars over which the charge change is measured. At least 1.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, standardise the change by the rolling charge std.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2, current_window is less than 1, or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.fields import market_current

result = market_current(df)
result.name                    # "market_current"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "market_charge.diff(current_window) / rolling_std(market_charge)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # -0.238304 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 147 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Positive values indicate the charge state is rising; negative values indicate it is falling; values near zero indicate a stable charge state. Descriptive only.

market_induction

from market_physics.fields import market_induction

market_induction(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the idea that induction arises from co-varying changes in coupled fields, this indicator measures how the change in volume participation and the change in signed motion move together over a window. When a rising volume field coincides with a rising signed motion (or both falling), the interaction is positive; when they oppose, it is negative.

The indicator is a **descriptive proxy only**: it is an induction-*style* feature, not physical induction, and not where price will go next.

core quantity rolling_mean(normalized_volume.diff() * returns.diff()) / rolling_std(normalized_volume.diff() * returns.diff())

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—Dataframe with close and volume columns.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, standardise the raw induction by its rolling std.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than 2 * window rows (the differenced fields and the outer rolling statistics stack their warm-ups).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.fields import market_induction

result = market_induction(df)
result.name                    # "market_induction"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "rolling_mean(normalized_volume.diff() * returns.diff()) / rolling_std(normalized_volume.diff() * returns.diff())"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.201132 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 99 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Positive values indicate volume-field and signed-motion changes co-move; negative values indicate they oppose; values near zero indicate weak interaction. Descriptive only.

polarity_index

from market_physics.fields import polarity_index

polarity_index(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    volume_weighted: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Treating each bar's direction as a polarity (+1 up, -1 down, 0 flat), this indicator reports the rolling balance of those polarities, optionally weighted by normalised volume. A value near +1 means up moves dominated the window; near -1 means down moves dominated; near 0 means they balanced.

The indicator is a **descriptive proxy only**: it summarises the recent directional balance, not where price will go next.

core quantity rolling_sum(sign(returns) * weight) / rolling_sum(weight)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—Dataframe with a close column (plus volume when volume_weighted is True).
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
volume_weighted kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, weight polarities by normalised volume.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in [-1, 1], NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value (2 * window - 1 when volume-weighted, since the volume weight needs a full window before the outer rolling sum; window + 1 otherwise).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.fields import polarity_index

result = polarity_index(df)
result.name                    # "polarity_index"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "rolling_sum(sign(returns) * weight) / rolling_sum(weight)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # -0.006007 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Values near +1 indicate up moves dominated the window; values near -1 indicate down moves dominated; values near 0 indicate a balanced directional mix. Descriptive only.

waves

Wave physics — Fourier analysis, resonance · 8 indicators

The superposition principle says any signal, however messy, is a sum of pure sinusoids.
