The discrete Fourier transform makes that decomposition computable: over a rolling window of returns it measures how much of the motion lives at each frequency, and the resulting power spectrum is the market's frequency fingerprint.

X(f) = Σt rt e−2πift   — DFT of the return window
S(f) ∝ |X(f)|²   — power spectrum: energy per frequency
Hspec = −Σ pf log pf / log K   — spectral entropy: how spread the energy is

Everything in the family is a reading of that spectrum. The dominant cycle period is the wavelength carrying the most power — the market's strongest rhythm right now. Cycle amplitude and wave energy measure how large and how energetic that rhythm is. Spectral entropy asks whether the energy is concentrated in a few clean frequencies (an ordered, cyclic market, entropy → 0) or smeared across all of them (noise, entropy → 1). Resonance flags windows where one frequency dominates its neighbours the way a driven oscillator rings at its natural frequency; phase shift tracks where in its cycle the market currently sits; and price–volume coherence treats price and volume as two coupled oscillators and measures whether they beat in sync.

Two practical warnings carry over from physics. Fourier analysis assumes the window is quasi-stationary, so regime changes mid-window blur the spectrum. And all periods are in bars, not clock time — a dominant cycle of 40 on hourly candles is a 40-hour rhythm.

When to reach for this family: rhythm detection — whether a cycle exists, how strong it is, and whether price and volume are locked together or drifting apart.

cycle_amplitudedominant_cycle_periodfrequency_pressurephase_shiftprice_volume_coherenceresonance_scorespectral_entropywave_energy

cycle_amplitude

from market_physics.waves import cycle_amplitude

cycle_amplitude(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 128,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
    min_period: int = 5,
    max_period: int | None = None,
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Physical analogy ———————— Once the strongest cyclic component of recent returns has been identified, its FFT magnitude describes how large that oscillation is. Cycle Amplitude reports the strength of the dominant allowed cycle.

For each rolling window the returns are demeaned and transformed with np.fft.rfft; the zero-frequency (DC) component is dropped. Among the bins whose period falls in [min_period, max_period], the largest magnitude is taken as the dominant amplitude. When normalize is True it is reported as a share of the total non-zero magnitude:

normalized_amplitude = dominant_magnitude / total_nonzero_magnitude

giving a value in [0, 1]. (Magnitude share is used, never power share.)

This indicator is **descriptive only**: it summarises the strength of the dominant recent cycle, not a buy/sell signal.

Interpretation:

* higher -> the dominant cycle accounts for more of the recent oscillation, * lower -> oscillation is spread more evenly across cycles.

core quantity dominant_magnitude = max(|rfft(demeaned_returns)|) over [min_period, max_period]; normalized = dominant_magnitude / total_nonzero_magnitude

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Only the close column is required.
windowint128Number of observations in each rolling FFT window (>= 8).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
min_period kw-onlyint5Shortest cycle period considered, in bars (>= 2).
max_period kw-onlyint | NoneNoneLongest cycle period considered, in bars. None imposes no upper bound; when provided it must satisfy min_period < max_period <= window.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, report the dominant magnitude as a share of the total non-zero magnitude (a value in [0, 1]); if False, report the raw dominant magnitude.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and for windows with no resolvable cycle.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window, return_method, min_period or max_period is out of range.
MissingColumnErrorIf the close column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.waves import cycle_amplitude

result = cycle_amplitude(df)
result.name                    # "cycle_amplitude"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "dominant_magnitude = max(|rfft(demeaned_returns)|) over [min_period, max_period]; normalized = dominant_magnitude / total_nonzero_magnitude"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.043328 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 128 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — higher means the dominant cycle accounts for more of the recent oscillation, lower means oscillation is spread across cycles

dominant_cycle_period

from market_physics.waves import dominant_cycle_period

dominant_cycle_period(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 128,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
    min_period: int = 5,
    max_period: int | None = None,
) -> IndicatorResult

Physical analogy ———————— A price series can be treated as a superposition of oscillations. Decomposing the recent returns with a discrete Fourier transform reveals which cyclic component carries the most power. The Dominant Cycle Period reports the length (in bars) of that strongest cycle.

For each rolling window the returns are demeaned, transformed with np.fft.rfft, and the zero-frequency (DC) component is dropped. Frequencies are converted to periods via period = 1 / frequency (so periods are measured in bars), filtered to [min_period, max_period], and the period with the maximum power is returned.

This indicator is **descriptive only**: it summarises the length of the strongest recent cycle, not a buy/sell signal, and implies no prediction that the cycle will persist.

Interpretation:

* larger value -> the strongest recent oscillation is a longer (slower) cycle, * smaller value -> the strongest recent oscillation is a shorter (faster) cycle.

core quantity period = 1 / argmax_frequency(power_spectrum(demeaned_returns)); DC ignored; restricted to [min_period, max_period]

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Only the close column is required.
windowint128Number of observations in each rolling FFT window (>= 8).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
min_period kw-onlyint5Shortest cycle period considered, in bars (>= 2).
max_period kw-onlyint | NoneNoneLongest cycle period considered, in bars. None imposes no upper bound; when provided it must satisfy min_period < max_period <= window.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and for windows with no resolvable cycle.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window, return_method, min_period or max_period is out of range.
MissingColumnErrorIf the close column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows (the first return is NaN, so a full valid window needs one extra row).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.waves import dominant_cycle_period

result = dominant_cycle_period(df)
result.name                    # "dominant_cycle_period"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "period = 1 / argmax_frequency(power_spectrum(demeaned_returns)); DC ignored; restricted to [min_period, max_period]"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 14.222222 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 128 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — larger means the strongest recent oscillation is a longer (slower) cycle, smaller means a shorter (faster) cycle

frequency_pressure

from market_physics.waves import frequency_pressure

frequency_pressure(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 128,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Physical analogy ———————— The spectral centroid is the "center of mass" of a power spectrum along the frequency axis. Frequency Pressure reports that centroid for recent returns: it shifts toward higher frequencies when energy sits in faster oscillations and toward lower frequencies when energy sits in slower ones.

For each rolling window the returns are demeaned and transformed with np.fft.rfft; the zero-frequency (DC) component is dropped and the power spectrum is formed. The centroid is:

centroid = sum(frequency * power) / sum(power)

measured in cycles per bar. When normalize is True it is divided by the highest non-DC frequency present in the window, giving a value in [0, 1].

This indicator is **descriptive only**: it summarises where spectral energy sits along the frequency axis, not a buy/sell signal.

Interpretation:

* higher (normalised) -> more energy in faster oscillations, * lower (normalised) -> more energy in slower oscillations.

core quantity centroid = sum(frequency * power) / sum(power) (DC ignored); normalized = centroid / max_frequency

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Only the close column is required.
windowint128Number of observations in each rolling FFT window (>= 8).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the centroid by the highest non-DC frequency present so values land in [0, 1]; if False, return the raw centroid in cycles per bar.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and for windows with no resolvable spectrum.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window or return_method is out of range.
MissingColumnErrorIf the close column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.waves import frequency_pressure

result = frequency_pressure(df)
result.name                    # "frequency_pressure"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "centroid = sum(frequency * power) / sum(power) (DC ignored); normalized = centroid / max_frequency"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.41795 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 128 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — higher normalised values mean more energy in faster oscillations, lower values mean more energy in slower oscillations

phase_shift

from market_physics.waves import phase_shift

phase_shift(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 128,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
    min_period: int = 5,
    max_period: int | None = None,
    degrees: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Physical analogy ———————— Two oscillations at the same frequency can be offset in phase. Phase Shift reports the phase relation between volume participation and price movement at the dominant price frequency of a rolling window.

For each rolling window the price signal (close returns) and the volume signal (volume percentage changes) are demeaned and transformed with np.fft.rfft; the zero-frequency (DC) component is dropped. The dominant price frequency within [min_period, max_period] is located, and the phase difference at that frequency bin k is:

phase_difference = angle(volume_fft[k]) - angle(price_fft[k])

wrapped to [-pi, pi] (converted to degrees when degrees is True).

This is a **descriptive phase relation only** — it is **not** a predictive lead/lag signal and makes no claim that volume leads or predicts price. It is descriptive only, not a buy/sell signal.

Interpretation:

* the value describes the phase relation between volume and price movement at the dominant price frequency, * a non-zero value describes an offset in that shared oscillation; it carries no predictive lead/lag meaning.

core quantity phase = wrap(angle(volume_fft[k]) - angle(price_fft[k])) at the dominant price frequency k within [min_period, max_period]

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires the close and volume columns.
windowint128Number of observations in each rolling FFT window (>= 8).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute the price returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns. The volume signal always uses percentage change.
min_period kw-onlyint5Shortest cycle period considered, in bars (>= 2).
max_period kw-onlyint | NoneNoneLongest cycle period considered, in bars. None imposes no upper bound; when provided it must satisfy min_period < max_period <= window.
degrees kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, report the phase difference in degrees ([-180, 180]); if False, in radians ([-pi, pi]).
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and for windows with no resolvable cycle.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window, return_method, min_period or max_period is out of range.
MissingColumnErrorIf close or volume is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.waves import phase_shift

result = phase_shift(df)
result.name                    # "phase_shift"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "phase = wrap(angle(volume_fft[k]) - angle(price_fft[k])) at the dominant price frequency k within [min_period, max_period]"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # -160.231959 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 128 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — describes the phase relation between volume and price movement at the dominant price frequency; a non-zero value describes an offset in that shared oscillation and carries no predictive lead/lag meaning

price_volume_coherence

from market_physics.waves import price_volume_coherence

price_volume_coherence(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 128,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
) -> IndicatorResult

Physical analogy ———————— Two oscillating systems are "coherent" when their energy is distributed across frequencies in a similar way. Price-Volume Coherence compares the spectral power distribution of price movement with that of volume participation: it is high when both concentrate their oscillation energy at the same frequencies and low when they do not.

For each rolling window the price signal (close returns) and the volume signal (volume percentage changes) are demeaned and transformed with np.fft.rfft. The zero-frequency (DC) component is dropped, each power spectrum is normalised into a distribution, and the coherence is their cosine similarity, giving a value in [0, 1].

This is a **coherence-style proxy**, not scipy.signal.coherence. It is **descriptive only**: it summarises how similar the two spectral shapes are, not a buy/sell signal.

Interpretation:

* higher -> price movement and volume participation have more similar spectral power distributions, * lower -> less similar spectral power distributions.

core quantity cosine_similarity(price_power / sum(price_power), volume_power / sum(volume_power)); DC ignored

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires the close and volume columns.
windowint128Number of observations in each rolling FFT window (>= 8).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute the price returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns. The volume signal always uses percentage change.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and for windows with no resolvable spectrum in either signal.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window or return_method is out of range.
MissingColumnErrorIf close or volume is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows (both signals lose their first row, so a full valid window needs one extra row).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.waves import price_volume_coherence

result = price_volume_coherence(df)
result.name                    # "price_volume_coherence"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "cosine_similarity(price_power / sum(price_power), volume_power / sum(volume_power)); DC ignored"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.354565 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 128 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — higher means price movement and volume participation have more similar spectral power distributions, lower means less similar distributions

resonance_score

from market_physics.waves import resonance_score

resonance_score(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 128,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
    min_period: int = 5,
    max_period: int | None = None,
) -> IndicatorResult

Physical analogy ———————— A resonant system concentrates its energy at a single frequency. The Resonance Score measures how much of the recent spectral power is captured by the single strongest allowed frequency:

resonance = dominant_band_power / total_nonzero_power

For each rolling window the returns are demeaned and transformed with np.fft.rfft; the zero-frequency (DC) component is dropped and the power spectrum is formed. Among the bins whose period falls in [min_period, max_period], the largest power is the dominant band power; the denominator is the total power across *all* non-DC bins. The result lies in [0, 1].

This indicator is **descriptive only**: it summarises how concentrated recent spectral power is at one frequency, not a buy/sell signal. It makes no claim that the resonance will persist.

Interpretation:

* higher -> one allowed frequency explains a larger share of recent spectral power, * lower -> power is spread more evenly across frequencies.

core quantity resonance = max(power over [min_period, max_period]) / total_nonzero_power (DC ignored)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Only the close column is required.
windowint128Number of observations in each rolling FFT window (>= 8).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
min_period kw-onlyint5Shortest cycle period considered, in bars (>= 2).
max_period kw-onlyint | NoneNoneLongest cycle period considered, in bars. None imposes no upper bound; when provided it must satisfy min_period < max_period <= window.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and for windows with no resolvable cycle.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window, return_method, min_period or max_period is out of range.
MissingColumnErrorIf the close column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.waves import resonance_score

result = resonance_score(df)
result.name                    # "resonance_score"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "resonance = max(power over [min_period, max_period]) / total_nonzero_power (DC ignored)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.08821 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 128 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — higher means one allowed frequency explains a larger share of recent spectral power, lower means power is spread across frequencies

spectral_entropy

from market_physics.waves import spectral_entropy

spectral_entropy(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 128,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
    base: float = 2.0,
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Physical analogy ———————— Treating recent returns as a signal, the power spectrum describes how energy is distributed across frequencies. Spectral Entropy applies Shannon entropy to that (normalised) power spectrum: it is low when energy concentrates in a few frequencies and high when energy is spread evenly across many.

For each rolling window the returns are demeaned and transformed with np.fft.rfft; the zero-frequency (DC) component is dropped and the power spectrum is converted to a probability distribution p_i. The entropy is:

H = -sum(p_i * log_b(p_i))

optionally divided by log_b(n_frequencies) (the number of non-DC frequency bins) to give a value in [0, 1].

This indicator is **descriptive only**: it summarises how dispersed the return spectrum is, not a buy/sell signal.

Interpretation:

* low -> spectrum concentrated in fewer frequencies, * high -> spectrum more dispersed / noisy.

core quantity H = -sum(p_i * log_b(p_i)); p_i = power_i / total_power (DC ignored); normalized H = H / log_b(n_frequencies)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Only the close column is required.
windowint128Number of observations in each rolling FFT window (>= 8).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
base kw-onlyfloat2.0Logarithm base for the entropy (> 1). 2 gives bits.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the entropy by log_b(n_frequencies) so values land in [0, 1].
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and for windows with no resolvable spectrum.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window, base or return_method is out of range.
MissingColumnErrorIf the close column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows (the first return is NaN, so a full valid window needs one extra row).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.waves import spectral_entropy

result = spectral_entropy(df)
result.name                    # "spectral_entropy"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "H = -sum(p_i * log_b(p_i)); p_i = power_i / total_power (DC ignored); normalized H = H / log_b(n_frequencies)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.867528 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 128 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — low means the spectrum is concentrated in fewer frequencies, high means it is more dispersed / noisy

wave_energy

from market_physics.waves import wave_energy

wave_energy(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 128,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
    min_period: int | None = None,
    max_period: int | None = None,
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Physical analogy ———————— By Parseval's relation, the power spectrum of a signal distributes its energy across frequencies. Wave Energy measures the spectral energy of recent returns, optionally restricted to a band of cycle periods.

For each rolling window the returns are demeaned and transformed with np.fft.rfft; the zero-frequency (DC) component is dropped and the power spectrum |rfft|^2 is formed. Behaviour depends on whether a period band is provided (i.e. at least one of min_period/max_period is set):

* **With a band** and normalize=True: the reported value is the selected band power divided by the total non-zero power, a share in [0, 1]. * **With a band** and normalize=False: the raw summed band power. * **Without a band**: the raw total non-zero spectral energy, *even when* normalize=True — normalisation is not applicable without a band and is ignored.

This indicator is **descriptive only**: it summarises spectral energy, not a buy/sell signal.

Interpretation:

* higher -> more oscillation energy (in the band, if one is given), * lower -> less oscillation energy.

core quantity power = |rfft(demeaned_returns)|^2 (DC ignored); band power / total_nonzero_power when a band is normalised, otherwise raw summed power

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Only the close column is required.
windowint128Number of observations in each rolling FFT window (>= 8).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'pct'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
min_period kw-onlyint | NoneNoneShortest cycle period in the band, in bars (>= 2 when provided).
max_period kw-onlyint | NoneNoneLongest cycle period in the band, in bars (<= window when provided). When both bounds are provided they must satisfy min_period < max_period.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True *and* a period band is provided, report the band power as a share of the total non-zero power (a value in [0, 1]). Ignored when no band is provided.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and for windows with no resolvable energy.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window, return_method, min_period or max_period is out of range.
MissingColumnErrorIf the close column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.waves import wave_energy

result = wave_energy(df)
result.name                    # "wave_energy"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "power = |rfft(demeaned_returns)|^2 (DC ignored); band power / total_nonzero_power when a band is normalised, otherwise raw summed power"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.878868 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 128 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — higher means more oscillation energy (within the band, if given), lower means less oscillation energy

turbulence

Fluid dynamics — flow, vorticity, cascades · 9 indicators

Fluid dynamics distinguishes two states of flow. Laminar flow is smooth and layered — a river gliding through a straight channel. Turbulent flow is churning and eddying — the same river through rapids. Which state a fluid occupies is governed by the Reynolds number, the ratio of inertial to viscous forces; and once flow turns turbulent, Kolmogorov's 1941 theory describes what happens: energy injected at large scales cascades down through eddies of smaller and smaller size until viscosity dissipates it as heat.

Re = ρvL/μ   — inertia vs. viscosity: laminar below, turbulent above
E(k) ∝ k−5/3   — Kolmogorov cascade: energy spectrum across eddy scales
I = σv / v̄   — turbulence intensity: fluctuation over mean flow

The market analogue treats directional price movement as flow and volatility as its turbulent fluctuation. Market flow and flow velocity measure the smooth directional component; turbulence intensity is the classic engineering ratio — how large the fluctuations are relative to the mean drift. A trending, low-volatility market is laminar; a violent, directionless one is turbulent. Vorticity captures local rotation — rapid back-and-forth churn with no net displacement. Viscosity is the market's internal friction: how strongly it damps an impulse (thick liquidity absorbs; thin liquidity lets it rip). The volatility cascade is the Kolmogorov idea made testable: compare volatility at long horizons to short ones and ask whether energy is flowing down the scale ladder — a hallmark of stressed markets. Eddy pressure and the two regime scores summarise where the tape sits on the laminar–turbulent spectrum.

When to reach for this family: volatility-regime diagnosis with directionality kept in the picture — not just "is volatility high" but "is the flow smooth or churning, and which way is the energy moving through scales".

eddy_pressureflow_instability_scoreflow_velocityflow_vorticitymarket_flowmarket_viscosityturbulence_intensityturbulent_regime_scorevolatility_cascade

eddy_pressure

from market_physics.turbulence import eddy_pressure

eddy_pressure(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    volume_weighted: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Eddy Pressure is the fluid-dynamics analogue of the agitation carried by eddies in a turbulent flow. It combines two descriptive proxies computed from the same (optionally volume-weighted) flow steps:

* vorticity — the rolling rate of flow-step sign changes (how often the flow reverses direction), and * intensity — the rolling dispersion of the flow steps relative to their typical magnitude (rolling_std / rolling_mean(abs)).

eddy_pressure = vorticity * intensity

Higher values describe flow that both reverses often and fluctuates strongly in magnitude; lower values describe smoother, steadier flow. This is a descriptive feature only — it is not a trading signal and carries no directional recommendation.

core quantity eddy_pressure = vorticity * intensity

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires close and, when volume_weighted is True, volume.
windowint50Rolling window for both components (>= 2).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
volume_weighted kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, weight each flow step by rolling_normalized_volume(volume, window).
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever either component is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing, or volume is missing when volume_weighted is True.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined output (2 * window - 1 when volume-weighted, window + 1 otherwise).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.turbulence import eddy_pressure

result = eddy_pressure(df)
result.name                    # "eddy_pressure"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "eddy_pressure = vorticity * intensity"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.555426 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values describe flow that both reverses often and fluctuates strongly in magnitude; lower values describe smoother, steadier flow. Descriptive only — not a signal.

flow_instability_score

from market_physics.turbulence import flow_instability_score

flow_instability_score(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 100,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    volume_weighted: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Flow Instability Score is a composite descriptive proxy that averages three turbulence-inspired characteristics of the (optionally volume-weighted) flow steps over a window:

* vorticity — the rolling rate of flow-step sign changes (how often the flow reverses direction), * intensity — the rolling dispersion of the flow steps relative to their typical magnitude (rolling_std / rolling_mean(abs)), and * acceleration — the typical bar-to-bar change in the flow steps relative to their typical magnitude (rolling_mean(abs(diff)) / rolling_mean(abs)).

The score is the row-wise mean of whichever components are finite, and is NaN when too few components are defined. Higher values describe flow that reverses, disperses and jerks more; lower values describe steadier flow. This is a descriptive composite proxy only — it is not a trading signal and carries no directional recommendation.

core quantity score = mean of the finite components {vorticity, intensity, acceleration}

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires close and, when volume_weighted is True, volume.
windowint100Rolling window for every component (>= 2).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
volume_weighted kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, weight each flow step by rolling_normalized_volume(volume, window).
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever too few components are defined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing, or volume is missing when volume_weighted is True.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined output (2 * window - 1 when volume-weighted, window + 1 otherwise).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.turbulence import flow_instability_score

result = flow_instability_score(df)
result.name                    # "flow_instability_score"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "score = mean of the finite components {vorticity, intensity, acceleration}"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 1.049775 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 198 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values describe flow that reverses, disperses and jerks more; lower values describe steadier flow. Descriptive only — not a signal.

flow_velocity

from market_physics.turbulence import flow_velocity

flow_velocity(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Flow Velocity is the fluid-dynamics analogue of how fast the market-flow level is changing. It is the first difference of the rolling market-flow level. When normalised, the difference is standardised by the rolling standard deviation of the flow level, so a unit of velocity is expressed relative to the flow's own recent variability.

Positive velocity describes the flow level rising and negative velocity describes it falling; the magnitude describes how quickly the flow level is changing. Because it measures the change in the signed flow level, a positive value can mean upward flow strengthening or downward flow weakening, and a negative value can mean downward flow strengthening or upward flow weakening. This is a descriptive rate of change only — it is not a trading signal and carries no directional recommendation.

core quantity velocity = diff(flow) / flow.rolling(window).std()

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires the close and volume columns.
windowint50Rolling window for the flow level, its standard deviation and the underlying volume normalisation (>= 2).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueApplies to both the flow level and the velocity. If True, the flow level is magnitude-normalised and the velocity is standardised by the rolling standard deviation of the flow. If False, the raw flow level and its raw first difference are used.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the result is undefined (e.g. zero rolling standard deviation of the flow when normalising).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close or volume is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined output (3 * window - 2 when normalising, 2 * window otherwise).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.turbulence import flow_velocity

result = flow_velocity(df)
result.name                    # "flow_velocity"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "velocity = diff(flow) / flow.rolling(window).std()"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # -0.238304 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 147 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Positive velocity describes the flow level rising and negative velocity describes it falling; the magnitude describes how quickly the flow level is changing. Because it is the change in the signed flow level, a positive value can mean upward flow strengthening or downward flow weakening, and a negative value can mean downward flow strengthening or upward flow weakening. Descriptive only — not a signal.

flow_vorticity

from market_physics.turbulence import flow_vorticity

flow_vorticity(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    volume_weighted: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Flow Vorticity is the fluid-dynamics analogue of local rotation in a flow. As an MVP proxy it measures how often the flow steps reverse direction within a window: the rolling rate of sign changes in the (optionally volume-weighted) flow steps.

A sign change is counted between two adjacent bars only when both flow steps are finite and non-zero, so zero or missing steps never manufacture an artificial reversal. Values live in [0, 1]: near 0 describes flow that keeps its direction (little rotation) and near 1 describes flow that reverses almost every bar (high rotation). This is a descriptive feature only — it is not a trading signal and carries no directional recommendation.

core quantity vorticity = rolling_count(sign(flow_step) != sign(prev_flow_step)) / rolling_count(valid adjacent non-zero flow-step pairs)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires close and, when volume_weighted is True, volume.
windowint50Rolling window over which to measure the reversal rate (>= 2).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
volume_weighted kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, weight each flow step by rolling_normalized_volume(volume, window). Weighting rescales the steps but does not change their signs, so it does not change the reversal count.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index in [0, 1], with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the window contains no valid adjacent pair.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing, or volume is missing when volume_weighted is True.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined output (window + 1 when volume-weighted, max(window, 3) otherwise).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.turbulence import flow_vorticity

result = flow_vorticity(df)
result.name                    # "flow_vorticity"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "vorticity = rolling_count(sign(flow_step) != sign(prev_flow_step)) / rolling_count(valid adjacent non-zero flow-step pairs)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.406667 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 50 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Near 0 describes flow that keeps its direction (little rotation) and near 1 describes flow that reverses almost every bar (high rotation). Descriptive only — not a signal.

market_flow

from market_physics.turbulence import market_flow

market_flow(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Market Flow is the fluid-dynamics analogue of net directional flow through a window. Each bar contributes a volume-weighted flow step (its return scaled by normalised volume); the window's net flow is the sum of these steps. When normalised, the net flow is expressed as a fraction of the total flow magnitude over the window, giving a value in roughly [-1, 1].

Positive values describe net flow toward higher prices over the window and negative values describe net flow toward lower prices; magnitudes near the bounds describe strongly one-directional flow while values near zero describe balanced back-and-forth flow. This is a descriptive feature only — it is not a trading signal and carries no directional recommendation.

core quantity flow = rolling_sum(returns * normalized_volume, window) / rolling_sum(abs(returns * normalized_volume), window)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires the close and volume columns.
windowint50Rolling window for the volume normalisation and the flow sums (>= 2).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, express net flow as a fraction of total flow magnitude over the window (roughly [-1, 1]). If False, return the raw rolling sum of the flow steps.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the result is undefined (e.g. zero rolling volume mean or zero total flow magnitude).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close or volume is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than 2 * window - 1 rows (enough for one defined rolling flow value).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.turbulence import market_flow

result = market_flow(df)
result.name                    # "market_flow"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "flow = rolling_sum(returns * normalized_volume, window) / rolling_sum(abs(returns * normalized_volume), window)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.061099 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Positive values describe net flow toward higher prices over the window and negative values describe net flow toward lower prices; values near zero describe balanced back-and-forth flow. Descriptive only — not a signal.

market_viscosity

from market_physics.turbulence import market_viscosity

market_viscosity(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    volume_weighted: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Market Viscosity is the fluid-dynamics analogue of a fluid's resistance to flow. It contrasts the net distance the flow travels over a window against the total path length of the flow steps: efficient, one-directional flow covers most of its path as net displacement (low viscosity), while choppy, back-and-forth flow dissipates its path with little net displacement (high viscosity).

viscosity = 1 - abs(rolling_sum(flow_step)) / rolling_sum(abs(flow_step))

Values live in [0, 1]: near 0 describes smooth, efficient directional flow and near 1 describes choppy, self-cancelling flow. This is a descriptive feature only — it is not a trading signal and carries no directional recommendation.

When volume_weighted=True this is the unsigned resistance complement of normalised market flow: 1 - abs(market_flow), since both share the same volume-weighted flow step and rolling sums. The unweighted variant provides the close-return-only configuration.

core quantity viscosity = 1 - abs(rolling_sum(flow_step, window)) / rolling_sum(abs(flow_step), window)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires close and, when volume_weighted is True, volume.
windowint50Rolling window for the flow sums (>= 2).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
volume_weighted kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, weight each flow step by rolling_normalized_volume(volume, window).
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the result is undefined (e.g. a flat window whose total path length is zero).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing, or volume is missing when volume_weighted is True.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined output (2 * window - 1 when volume-weighted, window + 1 otherwise).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.turbulence import market_viscosity

result = market_viscosity(df)
result.name                    # "market_viscosity"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "viscosity = 1 - abs(rolling_sum(flow_step, window)) / rolling_sum(abs(flow_step), window)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.938901 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Near 0 describes smooth, efficient directional flow (net displacement covers most of the path) and near 1 describes choppy, self-cancelling flow (little net displacement per unit path). Descriptive only — not a signal.

turbulence_intensity

from market_physics.turbulence import turbulence_intensity

turbulence_intensity(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    volume_weighted: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Turbulence Intensity is the fluid-dynamics analogue of the turbulence-intensity ratio — the fluctuation of flow relative to its mean magnitude. Each bar contributes a (optionally volume-weighted) flow step; the intensity is the rolling standard deviation of those steps divided by the rolling mean of their absolute value.

Higher values describe flow steps whose sizes vary widely relative to their typical magnitude (more turbulent, irregular movement); lower values describe smoother, more uniform flow steps. This is a descriptive feature only — it is not a trading signal and carries no directional recommendation.

core quantity intensity = rolling_std(flow_step, window) / rolling_mean(abs(flow_step), window)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires close and, when volume_weighted is True, volume.
windowint50Rolling window for the standard deviation and mean (>= 2).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
volume_weighted kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, weight each flow step by rolling_normalized_volume(volume, window).
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the result is undefined (e.g. zero rolling mean of absolute flow steps).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing, or volume is missing when volume_weighted is True.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined output (2 * window - 1 when volume-weighted, window + 1 otherwise).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.turbulence import turbulence_intensity

result = turbulence_intensity(df)
result.name                    # "turbulence_intensity"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "intensity = rolling_std(flow_step, window) / rolling_mean(abs(flow_step), window)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 1.365688 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values describe flow steps whose sizes vary widely relative to their typical magnitude (more turbulent, irregular movement); lower values describe smoother, more uniform flow steps. Descriptive only — not a signal.

turbulent_regime_score

from market_physics.turbulence import turbulent_regime_score

turbulent_regime_score(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 100,
    *,
    short_window: int = 20,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    volume_weighted: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Turbulent Regime Score is a composite descriptive proxy that blends several turbulence-inspired characteristics into a single roughly [0, 1] figure. Each component is placed on a comparable [0, 1]-ish scale before averaging:

* intensity — the flow dispersion ratio squashed by x / (1 + x), * viscosity — the flow-path resistance (already in [0, 1]), * vorticity — the flow-step sign-change rate (already in [0, 1]), and * cascade — abs(short_vol / long_vol - 1) squashed by x / (1 + x).

The score is the row-wise mean of whichever components are finite. Because the four components have different warm-up lengths, early defined rows may be the mean of only the components available so far (as few as min_valid_components) before all four contribute. Higher values describe more turbulent-looking flow across the blended characteristics; lower values describe smoother flow. This is a descriptive composite proxy only — it is not a classifier or trading signal and carries no directional recommendation.

core quantity score = mean of the finite components {intensity/(1+intensity), viscosity, vorticity, cascade/(1+cascade)} where cascade = abs(short_vol / long_vol - 1)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Requires close and, when volume_weighted is True, volume.
windowint100Long-scale rolling window for every component (> short_window).
short_window kw-onlyint20Short-scale window for the cascade component (>= 2 and < window).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
volume_weighted kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, weight each flow step by rolling_normalized_volume(volume, window).
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index in roughly [0, 1], with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever too few components are defined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf short_window is less than 2, short_window is not less than window, or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing, or volume is missing when volume_weighted is True.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.turbulence import turbulent_regime_score

result = turbulent_regime_score(df)
result.name                    # "turbulent_regime_score"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "score = mean of the finite components {intensity/(1+intensity), viscosity, vorticity, cascade/(1+cascade)} where cascade = abs(short_vol / long_vol - 1)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.488982 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 100 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values describe more turbulent-looking flow across the blended characteristics; lower values describe smoother flow. Descriptive only — not a signal.

volatility_cascade

from market_physics.turbulence import volatility_cascade

volatility_cascade(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 100,
    *,
    short_window: int = 20,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    mode: Literal['ratio', 'difference'] = 'ratio',
) -> IndicatorResult

Volatility Cascade is the turbulence-inspired analogue of an energy cascade across scales: it compares the volatility of returns measured over a short scale against the volatility measured over a longer scale. The comparison is reported either as a ratio (short over long) or as a difference (short minus long).

Values above 1 (ratio) or above 0 (difference) describe short-scale volatility running hotter than the long-scale baseline, and values below describe the opposite. This is a descriptive multi-scale comparison only — it is not a trading signal, carries no threshold labels, and makes no directional recommendation.

core quantity short_vol = rolling_std(returns, 20); long_vol = rolling_std(returns, 100); cascade = short_vol / long_vol

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. Only the close column is required.
windowint100Long-scale rolling window for the volatility baseline (> short_window).
short_window kw-onlyint20Short-scale rolling window for volatility (>= 2 and < window).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
mode kw-onlyLiteral['ratio', 'difference']'ratio'"ratio" reports short_vol / long_vol; "difference" reports short_vol - long_vol.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the result is undefined (e.g. zero long-scale volatility under "ratio").

Raises
ValueErrorIf short_window is less than 2, short_window is not less than window, return_method is invalid, or mode is not "ratio" or "difference".
MissingColumnErrorIf the close column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.turbulence import volatility_cascade

result = volatility_cascade(df)
result.name                    # "volatility_cascade"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "short_vol = rolling_std(returns, 20); long_vol = rolling_std(returns, 100); cascade = short_vol / long_vol"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.881584 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 100 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — A descriptive comparison of short-scale volatility against the longer-scale baseline. Higher values describe short-scale volatility running hotter than the long-scale baseline and lower values describe the opposite. Descriptive only — not a signal and no threshold labels.

thermodynamics

Heat flow, equilibrium, free energy · 9 indicators

Thermodynamics describes systems too complex to track particle-by-particle using a handful of macroscopic state variables — temperature, pressure, energy, entropy — and a few laws about how they trade against each other. Heat flows from hot to cold; entropy of an isolated system never decreases; and the energy available to do useful work is not the total energy U but the free energy.

F = U − T·S   — Helmholtz free energy: work you can actually extract
dQ/dt   — heat flow between market and its surroundings
η = W/Qin   — efficiency: directional work out per unit heat in

The market mapping assigns activity the role of heat: trading intensity injects thermal energy, and volatility is the temperature it produces. Thermal pressure and thermal stress measure how hard that heat pushes on the current price; heat flow tracks energy entering and leaving; heat dissipation is energy lost without producing directional motion — churn that goes nowhere. Equilibrium deviation and the disequilibrium score ask the zeroth-law question: is the system settled, with no net macroscopic flows, or is it visibly out of balance and still relaxing? The free-energy proxy is the family's most economical idea: of all the energy in recent activity, how much is actually available to move price — high activity with high disorder (large T·S term) is heat you cannot use. Thermal efficiency closes the loop: how much directional work the market extracted per unit of heat it consumed — a Carnot-style score for the tape.

When to reach for this family: exhaustion and imbalance analysis — distinguishing activity that does work (moves price) from activity that merely dissipates, and spotting when the system is far from equilibrium and must relax.

disequilibrium_scoreentropy_flowequilibrium_deviationfree_energy_proxyheat_dissipationheat_flowthermal_efficiencythermal_pressurethermal_stress_index

disequilibrium_score

from market_physics.thermodynamics import disequilibrium_score

disequilibrium_score(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 100,
    *,
    short_window: int = 20,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    bins: BinsSpec = 'fd',
) -> IndicatorResult

This composite blends three bounded readings of how far the market's thermal state sits from equilibrium: the absolute short-vs-long thermal-activity deviation, the magnitude of the entropy change, and the deviation of raw thermal pressure from its own rolling baseline. Each is squashed into [0, 1] via abs(x) / (1 + abs(x)) and the score is the mean of the defined components.

This score shares its disequilibrium and entropy-flow components with thermal_stress_index, so the two composites may be highly correlated; disequilibrium_score additionally emphasizes pressure-baseline imbalance.

The indicator is a **descriptive composite proxy only**: it is not a regime classifier, not a trading signal, and not a claim about what comes next.

core quantity mean of bounded(short - long activity), bounded(entropy.diff()), and bounded(raw_pressure - rolling_mean(raw_pressure)); bounded(x) = abs(x) / (1 + abs(x))

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with high, low, close and volume.
windowint100Long lookback window. Must be at least 10.
short_window kw-onlyint20Short lookback window. At least 2 and strictly less than window.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
bins kw-onlyBinsSpec'fd'Histogram binning rule or integer bin count for the entropy term.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in [0, 1], NaN during the warm-up period and wherever fewer than two components are defined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 10, short_window is invalid, return_method is invalid, or bins is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than 2 * window - 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.thermodynamics import disequilibrium_score

result = disequilibrium_score(df)
result.name                    # "disequilibrium_score"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "mean of bounded(short - long activity), bounded(entropy.diff()), and bounded(raw_pressure - rolling_mean(raw_pressure)); bounded(x) = abs(x) / (1 + abs(x))"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.048418 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 198 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate the thermal state sits further from its recent equilibrium across the components; lower values indicate alignment. Descriptive proxy only.

entropy_flow

from market_physics.thermodynamics import entropy_flow

entropy_flow(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 100,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    bins: BinsSpec = 'fd',
    base: float = 2.0,
    flow_window: int = 1,
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Treating the histogram Shannon entropy of the rolling return distribution as a disorder-like quantity, this indicator reports how that entropy changes over flow_window bars. A positive value means the return distribution became more disordered; a negative value means it became more concentrated.

The indicator is a **descriptive proxy only**: it summarises the change in a rolling distributional entropy, not where price will go next. The entropy is computed locally (no public entropy-family import).

core quantity diff(rolling_shannon_entropy(returns), flow_window)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—Dataframe with a close column.
windowint100Lookback window for the rolling entropy. Must be at least 10.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
bins kw-onlyBinsSpec'fd'Histogram binning rule ("fd", "sqrt", "sturges") or an integer bin count >= 2.
base kw-onlyfloat2.0Logarithm base for the entropy. Must be greater than 1.
flow_window kw-onlyint1Number of bars over which the change is measured. At least 1.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, normalise each window's entropy by log_base(non-empty bins).
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 10, return_method is invalid, base is not greater than 1, flow_window is less than 1, or bins is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + flow_window + 1 rows (returns lose the first row).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.thermodynamics import entropy_flow

result = entropy_flow(df)
result.name                    # "entropy_flow"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "diff(rolling_shannon_entropy(returns), flow_window)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # -0.000445 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 101 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Positive values indicate the return distribution became more disordered over the flow window; negative values indicate it became more concentrated. Descriptive only.

equilibrium_deviation

from market_physics.thermodynamics import equilibrium_deviation

equilibrium_deviation(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 100,
    *,
    short_window: int = 20,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Treating abs(returns) * normalized_volume * (high - low) / close as a thermal-activity quantity, this indicator compares its short-window average against its long-window baseline. A short-term average above the baseline is a positive deviation from the local thermal equilibrium; below it is negative.

The indicator is a **descriptive proxy only**: it characterises how far short-term activity sits from its longer-term baseline, not where price will go next.

core quantity activity = abs(returns) * normalized_volume * (high - low) / close; deviation = (short_state - long_state) / long_state

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with high, low, close and volume.
windowint100Long lookback window. Must be at least 2.
short_window kw-onlyint20Short lookback window. At least 2 and strictly less than window.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, express the deviation relative to the long state.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2, short_window is invalid, or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than 2 * window - 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.thermodynamics import equilibrium_deviation

result = equilibrium_deviation(df)
result.name                    # "equilibrium_deviation"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "activity = abs(returns) * normalized_volume * (high - low) / close; deviation = (short_state - long_state) / long_state"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # -0.14225 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 198 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Positive values indicate short-term thermal activity above the long-term baseline; negative values below the baseline; values near zero indicate alignment. Descriptive only.

free_energy_proxy

from market_physics.thermodynamics import free_energy_proxy

free_energy_proxy(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 100,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    bins: BinsSpec = 'fd',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the *form* of the Helmholtz free energy F = U - T * S, this indicator combines three local proxies kept on commensurate scales: an internal-energy-like term U (the rolling *mean* of volume-weighted squared returns), a temperature-like activity term T (return dispersion times mean relative range), and an entropy term S (the rolling histogram Shannon entropy of returns). U is a rolling mean rather than a rolling sum so that the T * S term is a meaningful fraction of U instead of being dwarfed by an ever-growing sum.

The indicator is a **descriptive analogy only**: it is not a true thermodynamic free energy. U is an internal squared-return energy computed locally, not the public energy.momentum_energy indicator; T is a temperature-like activity related to the statistical.market_temperature core but computed locally and not imported.

core quantity F = U - T * S

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with high, low, close and volume.
windowint100Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 10.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
bins kw-onlyBinsSpec'fd'Histogram binning rule or integer bin count for the entropy term.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide F by rolling_mean(abs(U)).
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 10, return_method is invalid, or bins is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.thermodynamics import free_energy_proxy

result = free_energy_proxy(df)
result.name                    # "free_energy_proxy"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "F = U - T * S"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # -0.215638 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 297 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate the internal-energy-like term dominates the temperature-times-entropy term; lower (or negative) values indicate the reverse. Descriptive analogy only.

heat_dissipation

from market_physics.thermodynamics import heat_dissipation

heat_dissipation(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 100,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    volume_weighted: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Heat dissipation is the exact complement of the internal thermal efficiency: where efficiency measures how much of the total absolute movement translated into net directional movement, dissipation measures how much was lost to back-and-forth (cancelling) motion. It shares the same internal efficiency helper as thermal_efficiency.

The indicator is a **descriptive proxy only**: it is 1 - efficiency, not an independent quantity, and not where price will go next. When volume_weighted=True and parameters are matched, this equals market_viscosity; it is exposed here as the thermodynamic dissipation complement and computed locally without importing that indicator.

core quantity 1 - net_over_total_movement_efficiency

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—Dataframe with a close column (plus volume when volume_weighted is True).
windowint100Lookback window for the rolling sums. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
volume_weighted kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, weight movement by normalised volume.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in [0, 1], NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.thermodynamics import heat_dissipation

result = heat_dissipation(df)
result.name                    # "heat_dissipation"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "1 - net_over_total_movement_efficiency"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.866875 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 198 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Values near 1 indicate the motion largely cancelled out (dissipative); values near 0 indicate net directional movement dominated. Descriptive only.

heat_flow

from market_physics.thermodynamics import heat_flow

heat_flow(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 100,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    flow_window: int = 1,
    volume_weighted: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Treating absolute price motion (optionally weighted by normalised volume) as a heat-like quantity, this indicator accumulates that quantity over a window into an internal heat-like component and reports its change over flow_window bars. A positive value means the internal heat-like component is rising; a negative value means it is falling.

The indicator is a **descriptive proxy only**: heat_flow is the *change in an internal heat-like component*, not physical heat transfer, and not where price will go next.

core quantity diff(rolling_sum(abs(returns) * normalized_volume, window), flow_window)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—Dataframe with a close column (plus volume when volume_weighted is True).
windowint100Lookback window for the internal heat accumulation. At least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
flow_window kw-onlyint1Number of bars over which the change is measured. At least 1.
volume_weighted kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, weight absolute motion by normalised volume.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2, return_method is invalid, or flow_window is less than 1.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.thermodynamics import heat_flow

result = heat_flow(df)
result.name                    # "heat_flow"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "diff(rolling_sum(abs(returns) * normalized_volume, window), flow_window)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # -0.004238 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 199 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — heat_flow is the change in an internal heat-like component (accumulated absolute motion). Positive values indicate the internal heat-like component is rising; negative values indicate it is falling. Descriptive only.

thermal_efficiency

from market_physics.thermodynamics import thermal_efficiency

thermal_efficiency(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 100,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    volume_weighted: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the form of a heat-engine efficiency (useful work over heat input), this indicator compares the net directional movement of a window against the total absolute movement it took to get there. A value near 1 means the window moved almost entirely in one direction (efficient); a value near 0 means the motion largely cancelled out (dissipative).

The indicator is a **descriptive efficiency proxy only**: it is a window-normalised net-over-total movement ratio, not a physical efficiency and not where price will go next. When volume_weighted=True and parameters are matched, this equals abs(market_flow(normalize=True)) and 1 - market_viscosity; it is exposed here as a thermodynamic efficiency framing and computed locally without importing those indicators.

core quantity abs(rolling_sum(movement)) / rolling_sum(abs_movement); movement = returns * normalized_volume

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—Dataframe with a close column (plus volume when volume_weighted is True).
windowint100Lookback window for the rolling sums. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
volume_weighted kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, weight movement and heat input by normalised volume.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in [0, 1], NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.thermodynamics import thermal_efficiency

result = thermal_efficiency(df)
result.name                    # "thermal_efficiency"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "abs(rolling_sum(movement)) / rolling_sum(abs_movement); movement = returns * normalized_volume"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.133125 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 198 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Values near 1 indicate net movement dominated the total absolute movement (directional/efficient); values near 0 indicate the motion largely cancelled out. Descriptive only.

thermal_pressure

from market_physics.thermodynamics import thermal_pressure

thermal_pressure(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 100,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing loosely from the idea that pressure rises when a system is stressed and expanding, this indicator combines three expansion/stress terms: how large the current return is relative to its own recent volatility (standardized return stress), how wide the intrabar range is relative to its recent norm (range expansion), and how heavy the volume is relative to its recent norm (volume expansion). Their product, smoothed over the window, is the raw pressure.

This is intentionally **distinct** from statistical.market_temperature: it is a standardized expansion/stress product, not the temperature-like dispersion product. It is a **descriptive proxy only** — a thermodynamic pressure-*style* feature, not physical pressure and not where price will go next.

core quantity rolling_mean(return_stress * range_expansion * volume_expansion) / rolling_mean(raw_pressure); return_stress = abs(returns) / rolling_std(returns), range_expansion = range / rolling_mean(range), volume_expansion = volume / rolling_mean(volume)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with high, low, close and volume.
windowint100Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the raw pressure by its rolling mean.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value (3 * window - 1 when normalised due to the second rolling mean, 2 * window otherwise).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.thermodynamics import thermal_pressure

result = thermal_pressure(df)
result.name                    # "thermal_pressure"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "rolling_mean(return_stress * range_expansion * volume_expansion) / rolling_mean(raw_pressure); return_stress = abs(returns) / rolling_std(returns), range_expansion = range / rolling_mean(range), volume_expansion = volume / rolling_mean(volume)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.976262 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 298 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate stronger pressure-like expansion/stress (large standardized returns with widening range and heavier volume); lower values indicate a calmer pressure state. Descriptive only.

thermal_stress_index

from market_physics.thermodynamics import thermal_stress_index

thermal_stress_index(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 100,
    *,
    short_window: int = 20,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    bins: BinsSpec = 'fd',
) -> IndicatorResult

This composite blends four bounded thermodynamic-style stress readings of a rolling window: raw thermal pressure, short-vs-long thermal disequilibrium, the magnitude of the entropy change, and the magnitude of the internal-heat change. Each is squashed into [0, 1] via abs(x) / (1 + abs(x)) and the score is the mean of the components that are defined.

This score shares its disequilibrium and entropy-flow components with disequilibrium_score, so the two composites may be highly correlated; thermal_stress_index additionally includes pressure and heat-flow components. Component scales can differ because the entropy-flow and heat-flow magnitudes are naturally smaller than the bounded pressure and disequilibrium components.

The indicator is a **descriptive composite proxy only**: it is not a regime classifier, not a trading signal, and not a claim about what comes next.

core quantity mean of bounded(raw_thermal_pressure), bounded(abs(short - long) / long), bounded(entropy.diff()), and bounded(internal_heat.diff()); bounded(x) = abs(x) / (1 + abs(x))

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with high, low, close and volume.
windowint100Long lookback window. Must be at least 10.
short_window kw-onlyint20Short lookback window. At least 2 and strictly less than window.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
bins kw-onlyBinsSpec'fd'Histogram binning rule or integer bin count for the entropy term.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in [0, 1], NaN during the warm-up period and wherever fewer than two components are defined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 10, short_window is invalid, return_method is invalid, or bins is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than 2 * window - 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.thermodynamics import thermal_stress_index

result = thermal_stress_index(df)
result.name                    # "thermal_stress_index"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "mean of bounded(raw_thermal_pressure), bounded(abs(short - long) / long), bounded(entropy.diff()), and bounded(internal_heat.diff()); bounded(x) = abs(x) / (1 + abs(x))"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.154134 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 198 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate a blend of stronger pressure, disequilibrium, entropy change and heat change; lower values indicate a calmer thermal state. Descriptive proxy only.

statistical

Statistical mechanics — temperature, criticality · 8 indicators

Statistical mechanics is the bridge between microscopic chaos and macroscopic order: it derives temperature, pressure, and phase transitions from the statistics of many small random motions. Its central move is to read macro state from fluctuation statistics — temperature is not a property of any one molecule but of the velocity distribution of all of them.

⟨E⟩ = ½kT per degree of freedom   — equipartition: variance ↔ temperature
C = dU/dT   — heat capacity: energy needed to change temperature
ξ → ∞ at Tc   — criticality: correlation length diverges at a phase transition

Reading returns as molecular velocities, market temperature is the equipartition estimate — the variance of the return distribution; market heat is the thermal energy in recent activity and heat capacity is how much of it a unit of temperature change absorbs — a market with high heat capacity shrugs off shocks. The distribution indicators read the shape rather than the width: tail pressure and return distribution pressure quantify how heavy and how lopsided the tails have become. The most physics-laden pair is criticality and phase transition score. Near a critical point a system exhibits universal warning signs: fluctuations at every scale, diverging correlations, slowing recovery from perturbations. The scores look for exactly those signatures in the return series — the statistical shadow a regime change casts before it happens. Volatility clustering, finance's own stylised fact (large changes follow large changes), is measured here as the temporal correlation of fluctuation magnitudes.

When to reach for this family: regime fragility. It reads the shape and correlation structure of recent fluctuations for signs the current phase is stable — or close to a transition.

criticality_scoremarket_heatmarket_heat_capacitymarket_temperaturephase_transition_scorereturn_distribution_pressuretail_pressurevolatility_cluster_score

criticality_score

from market_physics.statistical import criticality_score

criticality_score(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 100,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'pct',
) -> IndicatorResult

The Criticality Score is a composite, descriptive read on how "excited" the recent market regime is, blending four normalised components that each capture a different facet of agitation:
