core quantity positive_decay = max(abs(error.shift(1)) - abs(error), 0); rolling_sum(positive_decay) / rolling_sum(abs(error.shift(1))); error = normalized(close - rolling_mean(close))

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the positive-decay sum by the rolling sum of abs(error.shift(1)).
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in approximately [0, 1] when normalised, NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined (e.g. a zero rolling sum of abs(error.shift(1))).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than 2 * window rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.control import damping_efficiency

result = damping_efficiency(df)
result.name                    # "damping_efficiency"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "positive_decay = max(abs(error.shift(1)) - abs(error), 0); rolling_sum(positive_decay) / rolling_sum(abs(error.shift(1))); error = normalized(close - rolling_mean(close))"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.154786 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 99 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate more historical feedback-error magnitude decay (motion pulling the error toward zero); lower values indicate less decay. Descriptive only.

feedback_error

from market_physics.control import feedback_error

feedback_error(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    baseline: Literal['rolling_mean', 'rolling_median'] = 'rolling_mean',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Treating a rolling local baseline (mean or median) as a descriptive "set point", this indicator measures how far the current close sits from that baseline — a feedback-error-*style* reading. When normalised it is a z-score-style deviation relative to recent volatility.

This is a **descriptive control-theory-inspired feature**, not a control system implementation: it uses no PID controller logic, no automated control loops, and no control action, and it produces no trading signal.

With baseline='rolling_mean' and normalize=True, this equals stochastic.mean_reversion_pressure(source='price') at matched parameters; here it is interpreted as a set-point feedback error and also supports a rolling_median baseline.

core quantity raw_error = close - rolling_mean(close) then / rolling_std(close)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column.
windowint50Lookback window for the baseline and standard deviation. Must be at least 2.
baseline kw-onlyLiteral['rolling_mean', 'rolling_median']'rolling_mean'"rolling_mean" or "rolling_median" local baseline.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the raw error by the rolling std of close.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined (e.g. a zero rolling standard deviation).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or baseline is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.control import feedback_error

result = feedback_error(df)
result.name                    # "feedback_error"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "raw_error = close - rolling_mean(close) then / rolling_std(close)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 1.147139 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 49 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Positive values indicate the close is above its local baseline; negative values indicate it is below; values near zero indicate it is close to the baseline. Descriptive only.

feedback_loop_intensity

from market_physics.control import feedback_loop_intensity

feedback_loop_intensity(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    min_valid_components: int = 2,
) -> IndicatorResult

Blends three bounded close-only readings into a single feedback-loop-*style* intensity score: how far the close sits from its baseline (absolute feedback error), how strongly recent motion has been corrective (absolute correction strength), and how often the error crosses its baseline (crossing frequency). Each is bounded and the score is the mean of the defined components.

This is a **descriptive control-theory-inspired feature**, not a control system implementation: it uses no PID controller logic, no automated control loops, and no control action, and it produces no trading signal.

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
min_valid_components kw-onlyint2Minimum finite components required to score a bar. Between 1 and 3.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in approximately [0, 1], NaN during the warm-up period and wherever fewer than min_valid_components components are defined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2, return_method is invalid, or min_valid_components is not between 1 and 3.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.control import feedback_loop_intensity

result = feedback_loop_intensity(df)
result.name                    # "feedback_loop_intensity"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.236349 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate more active feedback-loop-style behaviour (larger error, stronger corrective tendency, and more frequent baseline crossings); lower values indicate quieter behaviour. Descriptive only.

overshoot_pressure

from market_physics.control import overshoot_pressure

overshoot_pressure(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

When the normalized feedback error crosses its baseline (a finite, non-zero sign change), the magnitude of the error just after the crossing measures how far price "overshot" the set point. Averaging those post-crossing magnitudes over a window gives an overshoot-pressure-*style* reading.

This is a **descriptive control-theory-inspired feature**, not a control system implementation: it uses no PID controller logic, no automated control loops, and no control action, and it produces no trading signal.

core quantity overshoot_raw = abs(error) on finite non-zero baseline crossings else 0; rolling_mean(overshoot_raw) / rolling_mean(abs(error)); error = normalized(close - rolling_mean(close))

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling means. Must be at least 2.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the rolling-mean overshoot by the rolling mean of abs(error).
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than 2 * window - 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.control import overshoot_pressure

result = overshoot_pressure(df)
result.name                    # "overshoot_pressure"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "overshoot_raw = abs(error) on finite non-zero baseline crossings else 0; rolling_mean(overshoot_raw) / rolling_mean(abs(error)); error = normalized(close - rolling_mean(close))"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.039224 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate larger post-crossing deviation magnitudes in recent windows; lower values indicate smaller overshoots. Descriptive only.

stability_margin

from market_physics.control import stability_margin

stability_margin(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
) -> IndicatorResult

The share of recent absolute movement that acted to *reduce* the prior bar's feedback-error direction gives a stability-margin-*style* reading: how much of the market's motion has, historically, been corrective rather than diverging.

This is a **descriptive control-theory-inspired feature**, not a control system implementation: it uses no PID controller logic, no automated control loops, and no control action, and it produces no trading signal.

At matched parameters, this equals (1 + correction_strength) / 2; it is exposed as a bounded corrective-movement share rather than signed correction strength.

core quantity rolling_sum(max(-sign(error.shift(1)) * returns, 0)) / rolling_sum(abs(returns)); error = normalized(close - rolling_mean(close))

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling sums. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in approximately [0, 1], NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined (e.g. a zero rolling sum of abs(returns)).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than 2 * window rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.control import stability_margin

result = stability_margin(df)
result.name                    # "stability_margin"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "rolling_sum(max(-sign(error.shift(1)) * returns, 0)) / rolling_sum(abs(returns)); error = normalized(close - rolling_mean(close))"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.55492 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 99 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate a larger historical share of movement that reduced the prior error direction (corrective); lower values indicate less corrective movement. Descriptive only.

geometry

Differential geometry — curvature, geodesics, time dilation · 9 indicators

Differential geometry studies curves and spaces by their intrinsic shape. A curve has a curvature — how sharply it bends at each point; a space has a metric — the rule for measuring distance within it; and the straightest possible path through a space is a geodesic. General relativity made this the deepest language in physics: matter curves spacetime, freely falling bodies follow geodesics, and clocks under stress run at different rates. The geometry family borrows this vocabulary to describe the shape of the price path itself.

κ ≈ |x″| / (1 + x′²)3/2   — curvature: how sharply the path bends
L / |Δx|   — path length over net displacement: tortuosity of the route
dτ = dt · f(σ)   — "market time" runs faster in high-volatility stretches

The shape readings are honest plane geometry on the discrete price curve. Price curvature is the second-difference bending of the path — near zero along a straight trend, spiking at V-shaped reversals — and trajectory bending and curvature pressure aggregate how much bending the recent path has accumulated. Geodesic deviation compares the distance price actually travelled (arc length) with the straight-line displacement it achieved: the gap is wasted motion, the fraction of the journey spent wandering off the direct route. Path efficiency is the same comparison expressed as a ratio — 1 for a perfectly direct path, falling toward 0 for pure churn. The relativistic metaphors extend the language: volatility time dilation treats rolling volatility as the rate at which "market time" passes — stressed markets live through more events per bar; metric distortion asks whether the market's distance measure itself is warping (volatility of volatility, in geometric dress); relative motion measures recent drift against a longer reference frame. The composite geometry stress summarises the path's overall geometric strain.

The module is unusually explicit about its epistemics, and the docstrings repeat it: these are geometry-inspired descriptive features. No actual relativity or spacetime model is claimed — and some raw modes coincide with simpler cousins (raw volatility_time_dilation equals stochastic.brownian_volatility at matched parameters; the geometric interpretation is the added value).

When to reach for this family: path-quality measurement — whether recent price action is a clean, efficient, gently-curving route or a tortuous, sharply-bending, stressed one, independent of its direction.

curvature_pressuregeodesic_deviationgeometry_stressmetric_distortionpath_efficiencyprice_curvaturerelative_motion_indextrajectory_bendingvolatility_time_dilation

curvature_pressure

from market_physics.geometry import curvature_pressure

curvature_pressure(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    min_valid_components: int = 2,
) -> IndicatorResult

Blends three bounded geometry-of-the-path readings into a single "pressure"- *style* score: how sharply the log-price path is bending (curvature), how much the return path zig-zags (bending/tortuosity) and how distorted the local metric is (standardized move size). Each is bounded and the score is the mean of the defined components.

This is a **geometry-inspired descriptive feature only**: no real relativity, spacetime, physics engine, or predictive model is involved, and it produces no trading signal.

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
min_valid_components kw-onlyint2Minimum finite components required to score a bar. Between 1 and 3.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in approximately [0, 1], NaN during the warm-up period and wherever fewer than min_valid_components components are defined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2, return_method is invalid, or min_valid_components is not between 1 and 3.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.geometry import curvature_pressure

result = curvature_pressure(df)
result.name                    # "curvature_pressure"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.5311 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 51 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate a more geometrically stressed path (sharper curvature, more bending, larger metric distortion); lower values indicate a calmer path. Descriptive only.

geodesic_deviation

from market_physics.geometry import geodesic_deviation

geodesic_deviation(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    price_column: str = 'close',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

The gap between how far price actually travelled (path length) and the straight- line distance it covered (net displacement) gives a geodesic-deviation-*style* reading: how far the realised path strayed from the direct "geodesic" route. When normalized it is the wasted-motion fraction of the path.

This is a **geometry-inspired descriptive feature only**: no real relativity, spacetime, physics engine, or predictive model is involved, and it produces no trading signal.

core quantity deviation_raw = rolling_sum(abs(price.diff()), window) - abs(price - price.shift(window)) then / rolling_sum(abs(price.diff()), window)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe containing price_column.
windowint50Lookback window for the displacement and path length. Must be at least 2.
price_column kw-onlystr'close'Column to treat as the price path.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the excess length by the path length.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in [0, 1] when normalised, NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the path length is zero.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2.
MissingColumnErrorIf price_column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.geometry import geodesic_deviation

result = geodesic_deviation(df)
result.name                    # "geodesic_deviation"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "deviation_raw = rolling_sum(abs(price.diff()), window) - abs(price - price.shift(window)) then / rolling_sum(abs(price.diff()), window)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.932053 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 50 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate the path strayed further from the direct route (more wasted motion); values near 0 indicate a near-direct path. Descriptive only.

geometry_stress

from market_physics.geometry import geometry_stress

geometry_stress(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    min_valid_components: int = 3,
) -> IndicatorResult

Blends four bounded geometry-of-the-path readings into a single stressed-path- *style* score: volatility "market-time" dilation, local metric distortion, geodesic path inefficiency and short-vs-reference relative-motion magnitude. Each is bounded and the score is the mean of the defined components. Curvature is intentionally excluded so this composite is not a near-duplicate of curvature_pressure.

This is a **geometry-inspired descriptive feature only**: no real relativity, spacetime, physics engine, or predictive model is involved, and it produces no trading signal.

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
min_valid_components kw-onlyint3Minimum finite components required to score a bar. Between 1 and 4.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in approximately [0, 1], NaN during the warm-up period and wherever fewer than min_valid_components components are defined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2, return_method is invalid, or min_valid_components is not between 1 and 4.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.geometry import geometry_stress

result = geometry_stress(df)
result.name                    # "geometry_stress"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.387215 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 99 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate a more geometrically stressed path across volatility-adjusted time, local metric distortion, path inefficiency and relative motion; lower values indicate a calmer path. Descriptive only.

metric_distortion

from market_physics.geometry import metric_distortion

metric_distortion(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    method: Literal['volatility', 'range'] = 'volatility',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the *metaphor* of a distorted metric (local distances stretching and shrinking), this indicator measures how large the current move is relative to its recent norm — either as a standardized absolute return ("volatility") or as a range expansion ("range").

This is a **geometry-inspired descriptive feature only**: no real relativity, spacetime, physics engine, or predictive model is involved, and it produces no trading signal.

core quantity distortion_raw = abs(returns) / rolling_std(returns, window) then / rolling_mean(distortion_raw, window)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. "volatility" requires close; "range" requires high, low and close.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns ("volatility" method only).
method kw-onlyLiteral['volatility', 'range']'volatility'"volatility" or "range" distortion source.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the raw distortion by its rolling mean.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2, return_method is invalid, or method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.geometry import metric_distortion

result = metric_distortion(df)
result.name                    # "metric_distortion"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "distortion_raw = abs(returns) / rolling_std(returns, window) then / rolling_mean(distortion_raw, window)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 1.163577 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 99 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate a locally 'distorted' metric (a move large relative to its recent norm); lower values indicate a calm, undistorted metric. Descriptive only.

path_efficiency

from market_physics.geometry import path_efficiency

path_efficiency(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    price_column: str = 'close',
) -> IndicatorResult

The ratio of net displacement to total path length over a window gives a path-efficiency-*style* reading: how directly price travelled from where it was window bars ago to where it is now (1 = a straight line, near 0 = a lot of back-and-forth for little net progress).

This is a **geometry-inspired descriptive feature only**: no real relativity, spacetime, physics engine, or predictive model is involved, and it produces no trading signal.

Equals 1 - fractals.roughness_index at a one-bar window offset when roughness_index is normalized (a points-vs-bars window convention); related to thermodynamics.thermal_efficiency as a price-path efficiency analogue.

core quantity abs(price - price.shift(window)) / rolling_sum(abs(price.diff()), window)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe containing price_column.
windowint50Lookback window for the displacement and path length. Must be at least 2.
price_column kw-onlystr'close'Column to treat as the price path.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in [0, 1], NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the path length is zero.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2.
MissingColumnErrorIf price_column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.geometry import path_efficiency

result = path_efficiency(df)
result.name                    # "path_efficiency"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "abs(price - price.shift(window)) / rolling_sum(abs(price.diff()), window)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.067947 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 50 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Values near 1 indicate price travelled almost directly over the window; values near 0 indicate a lot of back-and-forth for little net progress. Descriptive only.

price_curvature

from market_physics.geometry import price_curvature

price_curvature(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    price_column: str = 'close',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Treating the *log-price* path as a discrete curve over the bar index, this indicator measures its geometric curvature |acceleration| / (1 + velocity^2)^1.5 where velocity = path.diff(), acceleration = velocity.diff() and path = log(price) — how sharply the path is bending at each bar. Working on the log-price path makes the reading price-scale invariant (a constant multiplicative rescale of the price drops out under differencing).

This is a **geometry-inspired descriptive feature only**: it is not physical spacetime curvature and not a turn prediction.

core quantity curvature_raw = abs(acceleration) / (1 + velocity**2) ** 1.5 then / rolling_mean(curvature_raw); path = log(price), velocity = path.diff(), acceleration = velocity.diff()

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe containing price_column.
windowint50Lookback window for the normalising rolling mean. Must be at least 2.
price_column kw-onlystr'close'Column to treat as the price path.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the raw curvature by its rolling mean.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined (e.g. a zero rolling-mean baseline).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2.
MissingColumnErrorIf price_column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value (window + 2 when normalised, 3 otherwise).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.geometry import price_curvature

result = price_curvature(df)
result.name                    # "price_curvature"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "curvature_raw = abs(acceleration) / (1 + velocity**2) ** 1.5 then / rolling_mean(curvature_raw); path = log(price), velocity = path.diff(), acceleration = velocity.diff()"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 1.475564 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 51 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate the log-price path is bending more sharply; lower values indicate a straighter path. Descriptive only.

relative_motion_index

from market_physics.geometry import relative_motion_index

relative_motion_index(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    reference_window: int = 200,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the *metaphor* of motion measured against a reference frame, this indicator compares short-window mean return (recent "motion") against a longer reference-window mean return (the reference frame), optionally standardized by the reference-window return volatility.

This is a **geometry-inspired descriptive feature only**: no real relativity, spacetime, physics engine, or predictive model is involved, and it produces no trading signal.

core quantity rolling_mean(returns, window) - rolling_mean(returns, reference_window) then / rolling_std(returns, reference_window)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column.
windowint50Short motion window. Must be at least 3.
reference_window kw-onlyint200Reference-frame window. Must exceed window.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide by the reference-window return volatility.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined (e.g. a zero reference-window volatility).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 3, reference_window is not greater than window, or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than reference_window + 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.geometry import relative_motion_index

result = relative_motion_index(df)
result.name                    # "relative_motion_index"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "rolling_mean(returns, window) - rolling_mean(returns, reference_window) then / rolling_std(returns, reference_window)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # -0.074835 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 200 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Positive values indicate recent motion is faster/higher than the reference-frame motion; negative values indicate it is slower/lower; values near zero indicate they agree. Descriptive only.

trajectory_bending

from market_physics.geometry import trajectory_bending

trajectory_bending(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Summing how much the return direction changes bar-over-bar gives a return-path bending / tortuosity-*style* reading: how much the trajectory "zig-zags" relative to how far it travelled.

This is a **geometry-inspired descriptive feature only**: no real relativity, spacetime, physics engine, or predictive model is involved, and it produces no trading signal.

core quantity rolling_sum(abs(returns.diff())) / rolling_sum(abs(returns))

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling sums. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the direction-change sum by the sum of abs(returns).
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined (e.g. a zero rolling sum of abs(returns)).

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 2 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.geometry import trajectory_bending

result = trajectory_bending(df)
result.name                    # "trajectory_bending"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "rolling_sum(abs(returns.diff())) / rolling_sum(abs(returns))"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 1.212176 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 51 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate a more bent / zig-zagging return path (more direction change per unit of movement); lower values indicate a straighter path. For Gaussian i.i.d. returns, normalized values are often around sqrt(2), so readings near that level can occur without persistent directional structure. Descriptive only.

volatility_time_dilation

from market_physics.geometry import volatility_time_dilation

volatility_time_dilation(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the *metaphor* of time running differently under stress, this indicator treats rolling volatility as a "market-time" rate: high-volatility stretches are experienced as faster market-time, calm stretches as slower. It is simply rolling return volatility, optionally divided by its own rolling mean.

This is a **geometry-inspired descriptive feature only**: no real relativity, spacetime, physics engine, or predictive model is involved, and it produces no trading signal.

Raw mode equals stochastic.brownian_volatility(annualize=False) at matched parameters; normalized mode expresses that rolling volatility relative to its own rolling mean as a metaphorical market-time rate.

core quantity rolling_std(returns, window) / rolling_mean(rolling_std(returns, window))

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the rolling volatility by its rolling mean.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value (2 * window when normalised, window + 1 otherwise).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.geometry import volatility_time_dilation

result = volatility_time_dilation(df)
result.name                    # "volatility_time_dilation"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "rolling_std(returns, window) / rolling_mean(rolling_std(returns, window))"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.975417 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 99 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate faster metaphorical market-time (elevated volatility relative to its recent norm); lower values indicate slower market-time. Descriptive only.

experimental

Measurement metaphors — uncertainty, superposition, resonance · 9 indicators

Every other family in this library transplants an established physical formalism. This one is different, and says so in its name: it borrows measurement metaphors — mostly from the vocabulary of quantum mechanics and resonance physics — and applies them to ordinary rolling return and volume statistics. The metaphors organise intuition; the mathematics underneath is deliberately plain.

uncertainty bandwidth   — rolling volatility read as a measurement's error band
superposition score   — how mixed the up/down "state" of the window is
resonance proxy   — spectral power concentration around a fixed reference period

The measurement metaphors: uncertainty bandwidth reports rolling volatility as the width of an uncertainty band around the price reading; observer sensitivity asks how much that reading changes when the measurement window itself changes — fragile readings flag unstable regimes. The state metaphors: state superposition scores how ambiguous the recent directional state is — a window evenly split between up-bars and down-bars is "maximally mixed", a one-sided window has collapsed to a definite state; the transition probability proxy estimates, from recent history, how often the market has hopped between those states. The resonance metaphors: the fixed-cycle resonance proxy measures how concentrated the return spectrum is around a chosen reference period, cycle locking whether the market's dominant rhythm stays pinned to it, and resonance deviation how far it has drifted. Composite pressure and regime scores summarise the family.

The docstrings are unusually insistent, and the documentation should be too: these are experimental metaphorical proxies only. There is no physical quantum model, no Schumann or Earth-frequency interpretation (reference periods are bars, not Hz), and some indicators coincide with plainer cousins at matched parameters (state superposition equals 1 − |microstructure.order_flow_pressure|). Treat the family as a sandbox: name-brand intuitions, commodity statistics, explicitly provisional.

When to reach for this family: exploratory feature generation — ambiguity, measurement-stability, and fixed-cycle readings that complement the established families, clearly labelled as experimental.

cycle_locking_scoreexperimental_pressureexperimental_regime_scorefixed_cycle_resonance_proxyobserver_sensitivityresonance_deviationstate_superposition_scoretransition_probability_proxyuncertainty_bandwidth

cycle_locking_score

from market_physics.experimental import cycle_locking_score

cycle_locking_score(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 128,
    *,
    reference_period: float = 20.0,
    tolerance: float = 0.15,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    smoothing_window: int | None = None,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the *metaphor* of cycle locking, this indicator measures how often the rolling dominant cycle period stays within a tolerance band around a reference period, smoothed over a short window.

This is an **experimental metaphorical proxy only**: no physical quantum, Schumann, or resonance model is involved, and it produces no trading signal.

core quantity rolling_mean(locking_event, smoothing_window); locking_event = 1 if abs(dominant_period - reference_period) / reference_period <= tolerance else 0

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column.
windowint128FFT window length in bars. Must be at least 8.
reference_period kw-onlyfloat20.0Reference cycle period in bars (positive, resolvable).
tolerance kw-onlyfloat0.15Non-negative relative tolerance band around the reference.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
smoothing_window kw-onlyint | NoneNoneRolling-mean window over the locking events; defaults to max(2, window // 4) when None. Must be at least 1.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in [0, 1], NaN during the warm-up period and for flat/unusable windows.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 8, reference_period is not positive/resolvable, tolerance is negative, return_method is invalid, or smoothing_window is less than 1.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + smoothing_window rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.experimental import cycle_locking_score

result = cycle_locking_score(df)
result.name                    # "cycle_locking_score"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "rolling_mean(locking_event, smoothing_window); locking_event = 1 if abs(dominant_period - reference_period) / reference_period <= tolerance else 0"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.84375 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 159 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate the dominant cycle stayed near the reference period more often within the smoothing window; lower values indicate it did not. Descriptive only.

experimental_pressure

from market_physics.experimental import experimental_pressure

experimental_pressure(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    min_valid_components: int = 2,
) -> IndicatorResult

Blends three bounded experimental readings into a single "pressure"-*style* score: bounded return-volatility bandwidth, directional-state ambiguity, and sign-transition frequency. Each is bounded and the score is the mean of the defined components.

This is an **experimental metaphorical proxy only**: no physical quantum, Schumann, or resonance model is involved, and it produces no trading signal.

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column (volume optional).
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
min_valid_components kw-onlyint2Minimum finite components required to score a bar. Between 1 and 3.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in approximately [0, 1], NaN during the warm-up period and wherever fewer than min_valid_components components are defined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2, return_method is invalid, or min_valid_components is not between 1 and 3.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.experimental import experimental_pressure

result = experimental_pressure(df)
result.name                    # "experimental_pressure"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.625469 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate more combined experimental pressure across volatility bandwidth, directional ambiguity and transition frequency; lower values indicate less. Descriptive only.

experimental_regime_score

from market_physics.experimental import experimental_regime_score

experimental_regime_score(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    resonance_window: int = 128,
    reference_period: float = 20.0,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    min_valid_components: int = 3,
) -> IndicatorResult

Blends five bounded experimental readings into a single regime-*style* score: bounded return-volatility bandwidth, directional-state ambiguity, sign- transition frequency, observation-window sensitivity, and a fixed-reference cycle power/closeness reading. Each is bounded and the score is the mean of the defined components.

This is an **experimental metaphorical proxy only**: no physical quantum, Schumann, or resonance model is involved, and it produces no trading signal.

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column (volume optional).
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
resonance_window kw-onlyint128FFT window for the cycle component. Must be at least 8.
reference_period kw-onlyfloat20.0Reference cycle period in bars (positive, resolvable within resonance_window).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
min_valid_components kw-onlyint3Minimum finite components required to score a bar. Between 1 and 5.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in approximately [0, 1], NaN during the warm-up period and wherever fewer than min_valid_components components are defined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2, resonance_window is less than 8, reference_period is not positive/resolvable, return_method is invalid, or min_valid_components is not between 1 and 5.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.experimental import experimental_regime_score

result = experimental_regime_score(df)
result.name                    # "experimental_regime_score"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.398451 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate a more combined experimental-regime reading across volatility bandwidth, directional ambiguity, transition frequency, observation-window sensitivity and cyclic concentration; lower values indicate less. Descriptive only.

fixed_cycle_resonance_proxy

from market_physics.experimental import fixed_cycle_resonance_proxy

fixed_cycle_resonance_proxy(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 128,
    *,
    reference_period: float = 20.0,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the *metaphor* of a fixed reference cycle, this indicator measures how concentrated the rolling return spectrum is around a chosen reference period, combining the power share of the nearest FFT bin with how close that bin sits to the reference.

This is an **experimental metaphorical proxy only**: no physical quantum, Schumann, or resonance model is involved, and it produces no trading signal. The reference period is measured in bars, not Hz; there is intentionally no Schumann or Earth-frequency interpretation.

core quantity reference_power_share * closeness; reference_power_share = nearest_bin_power / total_non_DC_power, closeness = 1 / (1 + abs(nearest_period - reference_period) / reference_period)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column.
windowint128FFT window length in bars. Must be at least 8.
reference_period kw-onlyfloat20.0Reference cycle period in bars (positive, resolvable).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in [0, 1], NaN during the warm-up period and for flat/unusable windows.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 8, reference_period is not positive/resolvable, or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.experimental import fixed_cycle_resonance_proxy

result = fixed_cycle_resonance_proxy(df)
result.name                    # "fixed_cycle_resonance_proxy"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "reference_power_share * closeness; reference_power_share = nearest_bin_power / total_non_DC_power, closeness = 1 / (1 + abs(nearest_period - reference_period) / reference_period)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.009335 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 128 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate more spectral power concentrated in a bin close to the reference period; lower values indicate diffuse power or a distant nearest bin. Descriptive only.

observer_sensitivity

from market_physics.experimental import observer_sensitivity

observer_sensitivity(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    short_window: int = 20,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    statistic: Literal['volatility', 'mean_return', 'superposition'] = 'volatility',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the *metaphor* of an observation depending on how it is measured, this indicator reports how much a chosen statistic changes between a short and a long observation window — how "sensitive" the reading is to the window used.

This is an **experimental metaphorical proxy only**: no physical quantum, Schumann, or resonance model is involved, and it produces no trading signal.

core quantity abs(short_stat - long_stat) / abs(long_stat) [float-drift-guarded]; stat = rolling volatility at short and long windows

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column.
windowint50Long observation window. Must be at least 2.
short_window kw-onlyint20Short observation window. Must be at least 2 and strictly less than window.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
statistic kw-onlyLiteral['volatility', 'mean_return', 'superposition']'volatility'Which statistic to compare across windows.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the raw sensitivity by abs(long_stat).
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2, short_window is less than 2 or not strictly less than window, return_method is invalid, or statistic is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.experimental import observer_sensitivity

result = observer_sensitivity(df)
result.name                    # "observer_sensitivity"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "abs(short_stat - long_stat) / abs(long_stat) [float-drift-guarded]; stat = rolling volatility at short and long windows"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.109207 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 50 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate the statistic changes more between the short and long observation windows; lower values indicate a window-stable reading. Descriptive only.

resonance_deviation

from market_physics.experimental import resonance_deviation

resonance_deviation(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 128,
    *,
    reference_period: float = 20.0,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the *metaphor* of a resonance offset, this indicator measures how far the rolling dominant cycle period sits from a chosen reference period, in bars.

This is an **experimental metaphorical proxy only**: no physical quantum, Schumann, or resonance model is involved, and it produces no trading signal.

core quantity abs(dominant_period - reference_period) / reference_period; dominant_period = local FFT dominant non-DC period (bars)

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column.
windowint128FFT window length in bars. Must be at least 8.
reference_period kw-onlyfloat20.0Reference cycle period in bars (must be positive and resolvable within the window, i.e. between 2 and window).
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the raw deviation by reference_period.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and for flat/unusable windows.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 8, reference_period is not positive or resolvable, or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 1 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.experimental import resonance_deviation

result = resonance_deviation(df)
result.name                    # "resonance_deviation"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "abs(dominant_period - reference_period) / reference_period; dominant_period = local FFT dominant non-DC period (bars)"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.288889 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 128 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate the dominant cycle period is further from the reference period; values near 0 indicate it is close to the reference. Descriptive only.

state_superposition_score

from market_physics.experimental import state_superposition_score

state_superposition_score(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    volume_weighted: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the *metaphor* of superposition, this indicator measures how ambiguous the recent directional "state" has been: a window split evenly between up and down participation scores high (maximally mixed), a one-sided window scores low.

This is an **experimental metaphorical proxy only**: no physical quantum, Schumann, or resonance model is involved, and it produces no trading signal.

Equals 1 - abs(microstructure.order_flow_pressure) at matched parameters when using the same return direction and participation weighting; exposed here as a directional-state ambiguity score.

core quantity 1 - abs(positive_weight - negative_weight) / (positive_weight + negative_weight); weight = volume / rolling_mean(volume), up when returns > 0, down when returns < 0

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe. volume_weighted=True requires close and volume; volume_weighted=False requires close.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
volume_weighted kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, weight each bar by its normalised volume.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in [0, 1], NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the total directional weight is zero.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf a required column is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.experimental import state_superposition_score

result = state_superposition_score(df)
result.name                    # "state_superposition_score"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "1 - abs(positive_weight - negative_weight) / (positive_weight + negative_weight); weight = volume / rolling_mean(volume), up when returns > 0, down when returns < 0"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.975962 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 98 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Values near 1 indicate a directionally mixed (ambiguous) window; values near 0 indicate a one-sided window. Descriptive only.

transition_probability_proxy

from market_physics.experimental import transition_probability_proxy

transition_probability_proxy(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    state_definition: Literal['sign', 'threshold'] = 'sign',
    threshold: float = 0.0,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the *metaphor* of state transitions, this indicator reports how often the return "state" changes bar-over-bar within a window — a rolling transition frequency.

This is an **experimental metaphorical proxy only**: no physical quantum, Schumann, or resonance model is involved, and it produces no trading signal.

sign mode equals turbulence.flow_vorticity(volume_weighted=False) at matched parameters on data without flat bars; denominator semantics differ on flat bars. threshold mode adds an explicit neutral state.

core quantity rolling_mean(transition_event, window); state = sign(returns), transition when adjacent states finite and non-zero and differ

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling mean. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
state_definition kw-onlyLiteral['sign', 'threshold']'sign'"sign" or "threshold" state mapping.
threshold kw-onlyfloat0.0Non-negative neutral band half-width ("threshold" mode).
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with values in [0, 1], NaN during the warm-up period.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2, return_method is invalid, state_definition is invalid, or threshold is negative.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer than window + 2 rows.
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.experimental import transition_probability_proxy

result = transition_probability_proxy(df)
result.name                    # "transition_probability_proxy"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "rolling_mean(transition_event, window); state = sign(returns), transition when adjacent states finite and non-zero and differ"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.406667 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 51 — NaN during warm-up

Reading it — Higher values indicate the return state changed more often within the window; lower values indicate a more persistent state. Descriptive only.

uncertainty_bandwidth

from market_physics.experimental import uncertainty_bandwidth

uncertainty_bandwidth(
    df: pd.DataFrame,
    window: int = 50,
    *,
    return_method: Literal['pct', 'log'] = 'log',
    normalize: bool = True,
) -> IndicatorResult

Borrowing the *metaphor* of a measurement's "uncertainty band", this indicator reports the rolling return volatility as a bandwidth, optionally expressed relative to its own recent norm.

This is an **experimental metaphorical proxy only**: no physical quantum, Schumann, or resonance model is involved, and it produces no trading signal.

core quantity rolling_std(returns, window) / rolling_mean(rolling_std(returns, window)) [float-drift-guarded]

Parameters
nametypedefaultdescription
dfpd.DataFrame—OHLCV dataframe with a close column.
windowint50Lookback window for the rolling statistics. Must be at least 2.
return_method kw-onlyLiteral['pct', 'log']'log'How to compute returns. "pct" uses percentage change; "log" uses log returns.
normalize kw-onlyboolTrueIf True, divide the raw bandwidth by its rolling mean.
Returns

An IndicatorResult aligned to df index, with NaN during the warm-up period and wherever the estimate is undefined.

Raises
ValueErrorIf window is less than 2 or return_method is invalid.
MissingColumnErrorIf close is missing.
InsufficientDataErrorIf df has fewer rows than needed for one defined value (2 * window when normalised, window + 1 otherwise).
Example
# df: OHLCV dataframe (open, high, low, close, volume)
from market_physics.experimental import uncertainty_bandwidth

result = uncertainty_bandwidth(df)
result.name                    # "uncertainty_bandwidth"
result.metadata["formula"]    # "rolling_std(returns, window) / rolling_mean(rolling_std(returns, window)) [float-drift-guarded]"
result.values.iloc[-1]         # 0.975417 on the synthetic dataset
result.values.first_valid_index()  # 99 — NaN during warm-up

An additional energy module exists as an architecture stub (momentum_energy) and is excluded from the totals. Shared plumbing lives in market_physics.core; the exception hierarchy in market_physics.exceptions.

Charts are genuine library output on the reference dataset, downsampled for display. Signatures, parameter tables, and docstring text are extracted directly from the source.
